   TLSInnerPlaintext.content if the sender desires.  This permits
   generation of plausibly sized cover traffic in contexts where the
   presence or absence of activity may be sensitive.  Implementations
   MUST NOT send Handshake and Alert records that have a zero-length
   TLSInnerPlaintext.content; if such a message is received, the
   receiving implementation MUST terminate the connection with an
   "unexpected_message" alert.
```

This rule is specific:

- zero-length `application_data` is allowed
- zero-length `handshake` and `alert` inner content is not allowed

# Protected Record Length Uses a `+2048` Policy Instead of the TLS 1.3 `+256` Limit

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS accepts arbitrarily large records without any bound. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** enforce an internal protected-payload upper bound and rejects oversized records.
- But the enforced bound is `content + 2048`, which is wider than the TLS 1.3 RFC limit of `2^14 + 256`.
- Additionally, no dedicated receive-side branch was found that explicitly checks `TLSCiphertext.length > 2^14 + 256` and aborts with `record_overflow` as the RFC mandates.

# Duplicate Extension-Type Uniqueness Is Not Enforced as a Unified Hard-Fail Rule

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS never rejects a duplicate extension. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** enforce some extension rules: it rejects extensions not allowed in the current message, and it has a special placement check for `pre_shared_key`.
- But there is **no unified, explicit duplicate-type hard-fail check** in the generic extension checker — the bitmask is updated with `|=` (idempotent), so a repeated extension type is silently absorbed rather than rejected.
- In practice, a duplicate-extension probe still fails, but with `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_MAC` (late transcript/key mismatch) rather than an immediate duplicate-rule rejection.

Therefore this item is classified as **Not Satisfied**: the [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2) `MUST NOT` uniqueness rule is not enforced at the extension-parsing layer.

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2):

```text

## Summary

The implementation does not fully enforce the TLS 1.3 requirement that, when the server is able to provide a certificate chain whose certificates are signed with algorithms advertised by the client, it should select and send such a compliant chain.  
Static analysis shows that the certificate selection logic only matches the client’s advertised **CertificateVerify** signature schemes and key type compatibility, but does not evaluate the signature hash algorithms used across the configured certificate chain. # mbedTLS Does Not Strictly Enforce the `NamedGroupList` Lower Bound

## 1. Summary

The issue is about the `supported_groups` extension in TLS 1.3, specifically the `NamedGroupList` structure.

The core mismatch is:

- the TLS 1.3 specification requires `named_group_list<2..2^16-1>`
- mbedTLS client-side construction does not generate an empty list
- but mbedTLS server-side parsing accepts `named_group_list_len = 0`

So the implementation does not fully enforce the lower bound `2` on the receiving side.

This is the common underlying issue behind the related findings.

## 2. RFC Original Text

From [RFC 8446, Section 4.2.7](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.7):

```text
   When sent by the client, the "supported_groups" extension indicates
   the named groups which the client supports, ordered from most
   preferred to least preferred.
```

The `NamedGroupList` structure is defined in [RFC 8446, Section 4.2.7](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.7):

```text
      struct {
          NamedGroup named_group_list<2..2^16-1>;
      } NamedGroupList;
```

This means:

- `named_group_list` is a byte vector
- each `NamedGroup` occupies 2 bytes
- therefore the minimum valid length is 2 bytes, which corresponds to one complete group entry

So:

- `named_group_list_len = 0` is invalid
- `named_group_list_len = 1` is also invalid
- `named_group_list_len = 2` is the minimum valid value

### 3.1 Client-side construction does no

# mbedTLS Does Not Fully Match TLS 1.3 `legacy_record_version` Handling

## 1. Summary

This issue is about one specific field: the TLS record header field `legacy_record_version` in `TLSPlaintext`.

The problem has two related but distinct parts:

- mbedTLS does not fully ignore `legacy_record_version` in unencrypted TLS records
- mbedTLS does not implement a dedicated rejection for record versions lower than `0x0300`

These two behaviors correspond to two related findings.

The important point is that both findings concern the same on-wire value, but they test different aspects of the RFC:

- whether the field is used as a version check at all
- whether obviously too-low values are filtered out

## 2. RFC Original Text

### 2.1 Structure definition

From [RFC 8446, Section 5.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.1):

```text
     struct {
         ContentType type;
         ProtocolVersion legacy_record_version;
         uint16 length;
         opaque fragment[TLSPlaintext.length];
     } TLSPlaintext;
```

This is the exact record-layer field discussed in this issue.

### 2.2 Main TLS 1.3 rule: ignore the field

From [RFC 8446, Section 5.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.1):

```text
   legacy_record_version:  MUST be set to 0x0303 for all records
      generated by a TLS 1.3 implementation other than an initial
      ClientHello (i.e., one not generated after a HelloRetryRequest),
      where it MAY also be 0x0301 for compatibility purposes

## 1. Summary

This issue concerns TLS 1.3 record length validation and the required alert for oversized records.

The core mismatch is:

- TLS 1.3 requires endpoints to terminate the connection with a `record_overflow` alert when a received record exceeds the record-layer size limits.
- mbedTLS does reject oversized record content.
- However, the observed mbedTLS path returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD` and does not send or pend the `record_overflow` alert.

```text

      TLSPlaintext.fragment.  The length MUST NOT exceed 2^14 bytes.  An
      endpoint that receives a record that exceeds this length MUST
      terminate the connection with a "record_overflow" alert.
```

This means a plaintext record with length greater than `16384` must be rejected with `record_overflow`.

# ClientHello `supported_versions` Accepts a Trailing Byte

## 1. Summary

mbedTLS accepts a malformed TLS 1.3 ClientHello where the
`supported_versions` extension contains a valid `ProtocolVersion` followed by
one trailing byte.

The malformed payload tested was logically:

```text
supported_versions.extension_data = 03 03 04 00
                                  |  |-----| |
                                  |  TLS 1.3 |
                                  |          trailing byte
                                  versions_len = 3
```

This should be rejected as a syntax error because the ClientHello form of
`SupportedVersions` is a vector of 2-byte `ProtocolVersion` elements. A vector
length of 3 is not an exact multiple of the element size.

Runtime testing shows that mbedTLS still negotiates TLS 1.3 and sends a
ServerHello instead of terminating the handshake with `decode_error`.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 `ProtocolVersion` is 2 bytes

From [RFC 8446, Section 3.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-3.4):

```text
uint16 ProtocolVersion;
```

So each `ProtocolVersion` element occupies exactly 2 bytes.

### 2.2 ClientHello `supported_versions` is a `ProtocolVersion` vector

From [RFC 8446, Section 4.2.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.1), the ClientHello arm of `SupportedVersions` is:

```text
ProtocolVersion versions<2..254>;
```

This means the extension body is not arbitrary bytes. It is a TLS vector whose
elements are `Protoc

title: ClientHello `supported_versions` Accepts a Trailing Byte

# mbedTLS TLS 1.3 `certificate_authorities` Validation Gap

## 1. Problem Summary

In the TLS 1.3 `CertificateRequest` parsing path, mbedTLS accepts the `certificate_authorities` extension type, but does not implement dedicated parsing of its internal structure. As a result, the client does not explicitly validate:

- whether the `authorities` vector satisfies `3..2^16-1`
- whether each `DistinguishedName` satisfies `1..2^16-1`

In practice, malformed `certificate_authorities` contents can be received and ignored, instead of being rejected during handshake parsing.

## 2. RFC 8446 Source Text

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.4):

```text
4.2.4.  Certificate Authorities

   The "certificate_authorities" extension is used to indicate the
   certificate authorities (CAs) which an endpoint supports and which
   SHOULD be used by the receiving endpoint to guide certificate
   selection.

   The body of the "certificate_authorities" extension consists of a
   CertificateAuthoritiesExtension structure.

      opaque DistinguishedName<1..2^16-1>;

      struct {
          DistinguishedName authorities<3..2^16-1>;
      } CertificateAuthoritiesExtension;

   authorities:  A list of the distinguished names [X501] of acceptable
   certificate authorities, represented in DER-encoded [X690] format.
```

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 `certificate_authorities` is allowed in `CertificateRequest`

From `library/ssl_misc.h`:

[Originally reported privately by @LiD0209; after analysis we do not think this particular issue leads to a vulnerability, so we are tracking it publicly.]

# Some TLS 1.3 `extension_data` Parsers Accept Trailing Bytes Instead of Failing

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS fails to check extension boundaries at all. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** enforce outer extension-block boundaries in several places.
- But some inner TLS 1.3 `extension_data` parsers do **not** verify that the extension payload is consumed exactly.
- As a result, a malformed extension can be accepted when its valid prefix is followed by extra trailing bytes.

So the actual inconsistency is narrower and more precise than a generic "extension block integrity" failure:

- the outer extension block may be length-valid
- but some individual extension parsers still accept trailing data that should make the extension invalid

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Outer extensions block must be exact

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.1.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.2):

```text
   If negotiating a version of TLS prior to 1.3,
   a server MUST check that the message either contains no data after
   legacy_compression_methods or that it contains a valid extensions
   block with no data following.  If not, then it MUST abort the
   handshake with a "decode_error" alert.
```

This wording is about the outer extensions block, but it shows the [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.1.2
labels: component-tls13
title: TLS 1.3: Accept Trailing Bytes Instead of Failing

body_excerpt:
`mbedtls_x509_crt_info()` fails on some certificates with the error -10624 = `MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL` = "-0x2980 - X509 - Destination buffer is too small", even when given a very large output buffer. Inspecting the behavior, I can see that the problem is in `mbedtls_x509_dn_gets()`. It has an internal buffer `char s[MBEDTLS_X509_MAX_DN_NAME_SIZE]` where it writes a printable representation of each name. If the printable representation doesn't fit, it returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`. There are several problems here:

* `XXX_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL` means “if you try again with a larger output buffer, it will either work or encounter a different problem”. It's a bug to return this error if a larger output buffer won't help.
* `MBEDTLS_X509_MAX_DN_NAME_SIZE` seems to be an arbitrary limit. It's documented as “Maximum value size of a DN entry”, but I don't know where this comes from.
* `MBEDTLS_X509_MAX_DN_NAME_SIZE` seems to be intended to be the maximum length of a DN entry, but here it's applied to a printable encoding which can expand each byte to up to 3 characters, plus a little overhead for metacharacters. So I think the practical limit is actually around 80 characters.
* I don't understand why this function uses an intermediate stack buffer and reinvents byte-to-hex printing rather than writing directly to the output buffer. It uses `snprintf` calls which already check that there is enough room and would handle hex printing.

Observed with Mbed TLS 3.6.6.
labels: bug component-x509 size-s
title: mbedtls_x509_dn_gets fixed-size internal buffer too small, causes wrong error code

body_excerpt:
### Summary
In PSA-client-only builds (MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C disabled, e.g. NS-side mbedtls dispatching to a secure PSA service like TF-M), TLS 1.2 ECDHE handshakes fail because the premaster-secret union is sized below PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE.

The local ECP_LIGHT cascade does not run in this configuration, so MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES collapses to 1. The `_pms_ecdh` and `_pms_ecdhe_psk` members of `union mbedtls_ssl_premaster_secret` are declared in terms of that macro, so the whole union ends up ~1 byte in builds that have only ECDHE-RSA / ECDHE-ECDSA enabled (no PSK, no ECJPAKE).

ssl_tls12_client.c:2436 passes sizeof(premaster) to psa_raw_key_agreement(); it returns PSA_ERROR_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, mbedtls maps that to MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HW_ACCEL_FAILED and aborts the handshake. No memory corruption, just a deterministic handshake failure that any PSA-client deployment hits.

### System information

Mbed TLS version (number or commit id): 6a08a7eb7 (development HEAD) Operating system and version: Zephyr RTOS, any Configuration (if not default, please attach `mbedtls_config.h`): 
- MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C disabled 
- MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_CLIENT enabled (PSA dispatched to TF-M)
- MBEDTLS_KEY_EXCHANGE_ECDHE_RSA_ENABLED and/or MBEDTLS_KEY_EXCHANGE_ECDHE_ECDSA_ENABLED 
- PSK / ECDHE-PSK / ECJPAKE key exchanges disabled
- PSA_WANT_ALG_ECDH + at least one PSA_WANT_ECC_* curve Compiler and options (if you used a pre-built binary, please indicate how you obtained it): arm-zephyr-eabi
title: NS-side mbedtls (MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C=0) under-sizes _pms_ecdh buffer

body_excerpt:
Found an issue where mbedTLS silently drops all X.509 extensions when the `[3] EXPLICIT` wrapper tag is changed to `[1]` or `[2]`. Tested on mbedTLS 2.28.

RFC 5280 §4.1.2.9 says the extensions field must use tag `0xa3` (`[3] EXPLICIT`). If you take a valid cert and change just that one byte to `0xa1` (issuerUniqueID tag) or `0xa2` (subjectUniqueID tag), mbedTLS accepts the cert but produces a `mbedtls_x509_crt` with:
- `ext_types == 0`
- `ca_istrue == 0` 
- `key_usage == 0`
- `max_pathlen == 0`

All critical constraints just disappear. The cert DER bytes are still there but the parsed structure reports no extensions.

**The bug:** In `library/x509_crt.c::x509_get_crt_ext()`, when the tag isn't `[3]`, the function treats it as "extensions absent" and returns success:

```c
if( ( ret = mbedtls_asn1_get_tag( p, end, &len,
        MBEDTLS_ASN1_CONTEXT_SPECIFIC | MBEDTLS_ASN1_CONSTRUCTED | 3 ) ) != 0 )
{
    if( ret == MBEDTLS_ERR_ASN1_UNEXPECTED_TAG )
        return( 0 );   /* ← silently skips on wrong wrapper tag */
    ...
}
```

The OPTIONAL-skip on UNEXPECTED_TAG is correct when extensions are genuinely absent, but wrong when a *different* context-specific tag is present — that's a malformed cert, not an optional field.

**12 other implementations reject it:**
OpenSSL 3.x, OpenSSL 4.x, LibreSSL, BoringSSL, GnuTLS, wolfSSL, Botan, NSS, ANSSI x509-parser, rusticata x509-parser, rustls-webpki, briansmith webpki — all reject with parse errors. Only mbedTLS and BearSSL accept.

*
labels: bug component-x509 size-s
title: X.509 extensions wrapper tag mutation silently strips all certificate extensions

body_excerpt:
### Suggested enhancement

Currently, benchmark.c provides benchmarks for the legacy mbedtls APIs. As the default crypto API is becoming PSA based, also the PSA APIs shall be benchmarked and the results compared to the legacy mbedtls API's performance.

Additionally, there is currently no benchmark for actual end-to-end TLS throughput. It shall be benchmarked as well, not over the wire but simply using buffers to write to and read from.

In my benchmark experiments, I came to the following conclusions:

1. AES CBC ciphersuites without ETM are damn slow due to the constant time HMAC used to counter Lucky13. However, they are so slow that especially embedded systems might be stuck in doing almost nothing else than calculating HMACs. Hence, it would be good to enforce ETM on AES CBC ciphersuites as only countermeasure for Lucky13 and not support non-ETM CBC ciphersuites anymore.
2. NULL ciphersuites also use constant time HMACs, which makes them almost as slow as the AES CBC ciphersuites without ETM. However, I do not see why NULL ciphersuites would need constant time HMACs. Which attack is supposed to be countered by this countermeasure? If there is no valid reason to use constant time HMACs, use regular HMACs instead.
3. Compared to the legacy mbedtls APIs, PSA APIs are highly inefficient. On every crypto operation, key management operations are performed, for example, to lock the key slot and get the key, verify that a certain operation is allowed to be done with a key, ... E
labels: enhancement size-l component-test
title: Benchmark PSA API and TLS throughput

body_excerpt:
## Description

This is a reference pr that can be used to replicate a baseline test for the `mbedtls_test_move_handshake_to_state` and `mbedtls_ssl_tls13_fetch_handshake_msg`

The states of the machines in TLS1.3 path are 

	
### Client path

~~~~~~
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HELLO_REQUEST (start)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_ENCRYPTED_EXTENSIONS (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CERTIFICATE_REQUEST (optional, recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_FINISHED (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE (optional, send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (optional, send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_FINISHED (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_FLUSH_BUFFERS
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_WRAPUP
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_OVER
~~~~~~

### Server path

~~~~~~
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HELLO_REQUEST (start)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_ENCRYPTED_EXTENSIONS (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CERTIFICATE_REQUEST (optional, send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_FINISHED (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE (optional, recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (optional, recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_FINISHED (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_FLUSH_BUFFERS
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_WRAPUP
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_OVER
~~~~~~

And the argumentation of the function can be found here ->
title: [Do-Not-Merge] Testing mbedtls_test_move_handshake_to_state

body_excerpt:
## Description

Replace usage of `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` guards in library code in favor of `PSA_WANT`.

The problem being resolved with this PR is that `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` is still used to guard TLS/X509 code. This is fine as long as builtin drivers are compiled because PSA_WANT are "[translated](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/blob/088771225c20d21306b2a94170dd7edf610d8589/include/tf-psa-crypto/build_info.h#L154)" to legacy symbols. However in builds where `MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C` is not enabled (as for example in TF-M) this creates a big problem:
- user is not allowed to set `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` in the configuration header file
- there is no auto-translation

Resolves #10618.
Resolves #10619.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** [edit mpg] no - internal. [orignal Valerio] to be honest I don't know. On one hand I would say "no" because it was already impossible for the end user to set `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` at build time. On the other hand this might be a bugfix (in case of TF-M build, for example). I'll let the reviewer decide on what needs to be done here.
- [x] **development PR** not required because: this one
- [x] **TF-PSA-Crypto PR** provided https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/688
- [x] **framework PR** not required
- [x] **3.6 PR** not required because: no backport
- **tests** not required because: no new code to be tested
labels: approved priority-high size-xs
title: library: replace `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` with `PSA_WANT`

This is the 3.6 backport of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/684.

Quite a few conflicts:
- in 3.6 `USE_PSA_CRYPTO` is still an option

- in 1.x we moved to always storing RSA private keys in `priv_id` (which previously was only the case for opaque/wrapping contexts).

Still, the backport was pretty easy, but reviewers should probably be on the look-out for silly mistakes I may have made while resolving the conflicts.

body_excerpt:
In the TLS-Exporter for TLS 1.3 we mistakenly call PSA_HASH_LENGTH() on an mbedtls_md_type_t when it should be called on a psa_algorithm_t.

Fortunately, these two types have almost the same values, since we have previously aligned them to make conversion more efficient. As a result, PSA_HASH_LENGTH() produces exactly the same value when called on an mbedtls_md_type_t as with the equivalent psa_algorithm_t.

Thanks to this happy coincidence, fix a largely cosmetic issue (rather than a major functional bug).

## PR checklist

body_excerpt:
Resolve https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/issues/675

* Move feature requirement macros that need to be defined before including any system header to a new dedicated header `library/mbedtls_platform_requirements.h`.
* Redo feature requirement macros from scratch, so that they're simpler and justified. This could cause “exotic” platforms to break.

Done in previous pull requests:

* Simplify `timing.c` (in a way that's technically an API break, but come on, it's `timing.c` which is not used by any library module, only by test/sample programs). This removes the use of `gettimeofday()` (we already use `clock_gettime()` for `mbedtls_ms_time()`). https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10615
* Always use C99-compliant printf on MingW, removing the need for `MBEDTLS_PRINTF_SIZET` in `debug.h`. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10625

This is the last of a long chain of PR. Merge order:

1. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10615
2. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls-framework/pull/279 and https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10607
3. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10625
4. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/694
5. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10606 and https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10621

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [x] **development PR** provided https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10606
- [x] **TF-PSA-Crypto PR** provided https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypt
labels: approved size-s priority-high
title: Simplify platform requirements before 4.1

body_excerpt:
This is technically a backward incompatible change, since it used to be possible to enable MBEDTLS_TIMING_C without MBEDTLS_HAVE_TIME. However, MBEDTLS_TIMING_C was only implemented on Windows and on Unix-like platforms anyway, so this is not a reduction in portability.

Required for https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/694.

body_excerpt:
## Description

This is the 3.6 backport of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/687

Beyond contextual differences, some feature macros and error codes have been changed to adapt to 3.6. The commit structure of the original PR has been preserved, except the commit "Deprecate old macro and move to compat header" has not been preserved, and there are additional 3.6-specific commits at the end.

Indeed, just backporting #687 doest not fully resolve https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/issues/476 for 3.6 as it has a few buffers that development doesn't have:
```
% gcc -Iinclude -Icore -I drivers/builtin/include -Os -fstack-usage -c library/pk*.c && rm pkcs*.su
% awk '{print $2" "$1}' pk*.su | egrep '^([0-9]{4}|[5-9][0-9]{2})' | sort -rn 
2464 library/pk.c:712:12:import_pair_into_psa.constprop
1168 library/pk_wrap.c:1335:12:rsa_alt_check_pair
1120 library/pk.c:806:12:import_public_into_psa.constprop
```
After the next three commits though, we're down to:
```
624 library/pk.c:792:12:import_public_into_psa.constprop
```
which I think is acceptable: the 2kB+ stack buffers are gone, as well as the 1kB buffers, and the only remaining one is 528 bytes - using the same [target](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/issues/476#issuecomment-3852996548) as for development.

This missed a few buffers used only when `MBEDTLS_USE_PSA_CRYPTO` is enabled, which were addressed in later commits. As of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10609/commits/c7
labels: approved size-s priority-high
title: [3.6] PK: avoid large stack buffers in "to/from PSA" functions

body_excerpt:
## Description

Parse and store all certificate policy OIDs instead of rejecting non-anyPolicy OIDs with MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_FEATURE_UNAVAILABLE. Per RFC 5280 section 4.2.1.4, certificate policy enforcement is optional and should be left to the application. This aligns Mbed TLS behavior with OpenSSL, GnuTLS, and wolfSSL, which all accept certificates with custom/enterprise policy OIDs. Unknown policy OIDs are now rendered as numeric dotted strings (e.g. 1.2.3.4) in certificate info output.
                                                                                                                                                                       
Fixes #8123. Related to #8935, #4062, #7182.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            

## PR checklist

  - changelog provided
  - development PR provided # | not required because: this is the development PR
  - TF-PSA-Crypto PR not required because: no changes to TF-PSA-Crypto
  - framework PR not required
  - 3.6 PR not required because: should be discussed with maintainers for backport (v3.6.5 backport available here as reference https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/mbedtls/pull/80)
  - tests provided

### Notes

---
labels: needs-review component-x509 size-s priority-medium
title: x509: Parse all certificate policy OIDs per RFC 5280

body_excerpt:
Resolves https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/10534

Add unused fields to some structs so that we can use them if needed within the lifetime of the 4.1 LTS. Add a union of a `size_t` and a `void *`, allowing us to use either type to extend the structure.

The structs with added fields are:
* `mbedtls_ssl_session`
* `mbedtls_ssl_config`
* `mbedtls_ssl_context`
* `mbedtls_x509_crt`

body_excerpt:
Support configuration of wildcard hostname against which common name of certificates can be verified.

## Description

Validate wildcard hostname against certificate CN. Perform domain suffix matching for certificates. This allows a configured CN (eg., "[any].example.com") to match any subdomain like "sub.example.com", "api.example.com", etc. The function extracts the domain portion from the provided hostname (everything after the first dot) and compares it case-insensitively against the CN suffix present in the certificate.

body_excerpt:
## Description

Fixes #10668

Fix a TLS 1.2 client regression introduced by the signature-algorithm validation added in commit 9f19fe1874d9e48ac56c7b4a65bdd95c0dee236c.

The new validation correctly rejects signature algorithms that are invalid for TLS 1.2 or were not offered by the client, but it also rejects valid `rsa_pss_rsae_*` SignatureScheme values even when RSA-PSS support is compiled in. As a result, handshakes with TLS 1.2 servers using RSA-PSS signatures can fail with `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER` (`-0x6600`).

This PR updates the TLS 1.2 support check to accept `rsa_pss_rsae_sha256/384/512` when the corresponding RSA-PSS and hash support is available, and adds regression tests for these values.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [x] **development PR** provided here
- [x] **TF-PSA-Crypto PR** not required because: no changes
- [x] **framework PR** not required
- [x] **4.1 PR** provided #10704
- [x] **3.6 PR** provided #10674
- [x] **tests** provided
labels: needs-review needs-backports priority-high size-xs

## Description

`ssl_tls13_client_hello_has_exts()` is called from three wrapper functions, each guarded by different `MBEDTLS_SSL_TLS1_3_KEY_EXCHANGE_MODE_*_ENABLED` preprocessor conditions. In configurations where none of those modes are active (e.g., builds that compile the TLS 1.3 server source but only use TLS 1.2 at runtime), the function is unreferenced and triggers `-Wunused-function`.

Add `__attribute__((unused))` to suppress the warning without removing the function.

## PR checklist

- [ ] **changelog** not required because: trivial warning fix, no functional change
- [ ] **framework PR** not required

- [ ] **mbedtls 4.1 PR** not required because: targeting development
- [ ] **mbedtls 3.6 PR** not required because: targeting development
- **tests** not required because: no functional change, build-only fix

body_excerpt:
## Description

TLS handshake messages such as Certificate can exceed `MBEDTLS_SSL_IN_CONTENT_LEN` (16KB) when servers use certificate chains with many intermediate CAs or certificates with many SANs. The existing handshake defragmentation reassembles fragmented messages in-place, but is limited to the compile-time buffer size — connections fail with "requesting more data than fits" when the reassembled message exceeds the buffer.

This PR adds a new compile-time option `MBEDTLS_SSL_TLS_HS_LARGE_MSG` that extends the existing in-place reassembly with dynamic buffer resizing. When enabled and configured at runtime via `mbedtls_ssl_conf_max_handshake_msg_len()`, the input buffer is proactively enlarged at the initial handshake fragment if the message will not fit.

Related to #1840 (this addresses the case where the reassembled handshake message exceeds the buffer size; the basic fragmentation support was added in PR #9872).

### Design

- Extends the existing in-place reassembly — no separate buffer or new reassembly path
- Buffer is resized at the initial fragment, before the reassembly loop begins
- Allocates to the configured max once; the enlarged buffer remains for the lifetime of the SSL context
- Only triggers when the message exceeds `MBEDTLS_SSL_IN_CONTENT_LEN` — no cost for normal-sized messages
- TLS-only (not DTLS)
- This feature serves the opposite purpose of `MBEDTLS_SSL_VARIABLE_BUFFER_LENGTH` (which shrinks buffers); they should be considered mut
title: Add MBEDTLS_SSL_TLS_HS_LARGE_MSG for large handshake message reassembly

## Description

This PR hardens TLS 1.3 serialized session loading.

The TLS 1.3 session loader now rejects malformed serialized string fields before passing them to string-based setters. It also rejects serialized TLS 1.3 sessions with trailing bytes, so loading requires full consumption of the serialized session data.

Progresses #10682

body_excerpt:
## Description

`MBEDTLS_GCC_VERSION` is only defined for GCC in `build_info.h` (excluded for clang via `!defined(__clang__)`). Lines 284 and 287 of `alignment.h` used it in bare `#if` directives without a `defined()` guard. When compiling with clang and `-Wundef`, the undefined macro triggers warnings through the include chain `common.h` → `build_info.h` → `alignment.h`.

Add `defined(MBEDTLS_COMPILER_IS_GCC)` guard to both lines.

### Reproduce

```
clang -fsyntax-only -Wundef -Werror \
    -I include -I library \
    library/common.h
```

### Testing

Build and SSL test suites pass.

A corresponding fix for the development branch has been submitted as Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto#([add PR number](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/782)).

## PR checklist

- [ ] **changelog** not required because: trivial warning fix, no behavior change
- [ ] **framework PR** not required
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto development PR** provided Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto#(add PR number)
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto 1.1 PR** not required because: can backport after merge
- [ ] **mbedtls development PR** not required because: file is in tf-psa-crypto submodule on development
- [ ] **mbedtls 4.1 PR** not required because: file is in tf-psa-crypto submodule
- [x] **mbedtls 3.6 PR** provided (this PR)
- **tests** not required because: compile-time warning fix only, no runtime behavior change
title: Fix -Wundef warnings for MBEDTLS_GCC_VERSION in alignment.h

body_excerpt:
## Description

`MBEDTLS_PSA_MAX_EC_PUBKEY_LENGTH` and `MBEDTLS_PSA_MAX_EC_KEY_PAIR_LENGTH` are only defined when `MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_CLIENT` is enabled (via `psa_util_internal.h`). However, the `PK_EXPORT_KEYS_ON_THE_STACK` block in `pk_internal.h` used them unconditionally, triggering `-Wundef` warnings when compiling with a config that does not enable PSA.

Guard the PSA-specific size macros with `#if defined(MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_CLIENT)`, falling back to 0 when PSA is disabled. The subsequent `MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES` and `MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_PT_LEN` comparisons then set the proper buffer size.

This issue does not affect the development branch, where `psa_util_internal.h` is included unconditionally.

### Reproduce

Create a minimal config without PSA (e.g., AES + SHA256 only):


```
cat  /tmp/minimal_config.h

#define MBEDTLS_AES_C
#define MBEDTLS_SHA256_C
#define MBEDTLS_CIPHER_C
```

```
gcc -fsyntax-only -Wundef -Werror \
    -DMBEDTLS_CONFIG_FILE='"/tmp/minimal_config.h"' \
    -I include -I library \
    library/pk_ecc.c
```

### Testing

Build and SSL test suites pass with default config.

## PR checklist

- [ ] **changelog** not required because: trivial warning fix, no behavior change
- [ ] **framework PR** not required
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto development PR** not required because: not affected (psa_util_internal.h included unconditionally)
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto 1.1 PR** not required because: not affected
- [ ] **mbedtls development P
title: Fix -Wundef warnings for PSA macros in pk_internal.h

## Description

Resolve failure caused by empty digest algorithm depends https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/10480

This PR is part of a two part set which must be merged in the following order:

body_excerpt:
In PSA-client-only builds (MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C disabled, e.g. NS-side mbedtls dispatching to a secure PSA service like TF-M), the local ECP_LIGHT cascade does not run and MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES collapses to 1. The `_pms_ecdh` and `_pms_ecdhe_psk` members of `union mbedtls_ssl_premaster_secret` then declare 1-byte arrays. In builds where only ECDHE-RSA / ECDHE-ECDSA is enabled (no PSK, no ECJPAKE), the whole union ends up that small, and psa_raw_key_agreement() returns PSA_ERROR_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL during the TLS 1.2 ECDHE handshake. The handshake aborts as MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HW_ACCEL_FAILED with no path to success in this configuration.

Introduce MBEDTLS_SSL_PMS_ECDH_SIZE as max(PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE, MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES) so both legacy ECP and PSA-client builds get a sufficient buffer. PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE is always defined, sized by the enabled PSA_WANT_ECC_* curves.

Fixes #10757.

## Description

Fixes TLS 1.2 ECDHE handshakes silently failing on PSA-client-only Mbed TLS builds (NS-side dispatching to a secure PSA provider such as TF-M). In that configuration `MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES` collapses to 1, sizing the premaster-secret union below `PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE`, so `psa_raw_key_agreement()` returns `PSA_ERROR_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL` during the key-exchange step. The fix introduces `MBEDTLS_SSL_PMS_ECDH_SIZE` taking the max of the legacy and PSA sizing macros, so both legacy ECP and PSA-client builds get a sufficient buffer w
title: ssl: size _pms_ecdh against PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE

body_excerpt:
## Description

Backport of #10715 to the `mbedtls-4.1` branch.

The deserialization paths for TLS 1.3 sessions and SSL contexts have been
hardened to reject malformed input as a second line of defence (the primary
protection remains application-side integrity protection of serialized data):

* `ssl_tls13_session_load` now rejects ALPN and hostname fields that are not
  null-terminated within the announced length, and rejects buffers that have
  unconsumed trailing data after the full record.
* `ssl_context_load` now rejects serialized SSL contexts whose DTLS Connection
  ID length exceeds the maximum supported size, instead of overflowing the
  internal buffer.

New tests cover all four error paths.

Related: CVE-2026-34877 (mbedtls-security-advisory-2026-03-serialized-data); see #10682, #10683, #10715.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [x] **development PR** provided #10715
- [x] **3.6 PR** provided #10762
- [x] **4.1 PR** provided here
- [x] **tests** provided
labels: enhancement needs-review component-tls13 priority-high
title: Backport to 4.1: harden serialized data deserialization

body_excerpt:
## Description

Backport of #10715 to the `mbedtls-3.6` branch.

The deserialization paths for TLS 1.3 sessions and SSL contexts have been
hardened to reject malformed input as a second line of defence (the primary
protection remains application-side integrity protection of serialized data):

* `ssl_tls13_session_load` now rejects ALPN and hostname fields that are not
  null-terminated within the announced length, and rejects buffers that have
  unconsumed trailing data after the full record.
* `ssl_context_load` now rejects serialized SSL contexts whose DTLS Connection
  ID length exceeds the maximum supported size, instead of overflowing the
  internal buffer.

The TLS 1.3 session-load tests from #10715 are included. The dedicated
CID-length test relies on test helpers introduced after 3.6
(`mbedtls_test_ssl_dtls_join_endpoints`, `mbedtls_test_ssl_perform_connection`)
and is omitted from this backport. The CID bounds check itself is unchanged.

This supersedes #10683.

Related: CVE-2026-34877 (mbedtls-security-advisory-2026-03-serialized-data); see #10682, #10715, #10761.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [x] **development PR** provided #10715
- [x] **3.6 PR** provided here (supersedes #10683)
- [x] **4.1 PR** provided #10761
- [x] **tests** provided (TLS 1.3 session-load mutation tests; CID test omitted, see description)

## Description

Instead of assuming tf-psa-crypto is always a subdirectory of mbedtls, allow its path to be specified.

This PR focuses only on the build system and mostly on CMake. Tests are not included as this would require updating all scripts (bash, python, perl) in this repo and in the `framework`.

## Rationale

This change, especially the CMake part, is useful when Mbed TLS and TF-PSA-Crypto are integrated in external environments like Zephyr where handling git submodules might be tricky:

1. Zephyr applies some patches on each git repo in order to adjust them for its internal purposes. Using git submodules in this case would mean changing the remote and the reference being used for the submodule.
2. Zephyr relies on `west` as repo management tool. In theory `west` supports git submodules, but doing so adds would add a new management tool on top of what's already being used in Zephyr.

## PR checklist

- [ ] **changelog** not required because: the feature is not completed (because of the test limitations) so it's better not to advertise it IMO.

body_excerpt:
## Description

Fixes #10753.

`mbedtls_x509_dn_gets()` rendered each DN value into a fixed 256-byte internal buffer before copying it to the caller's buffer. A value whose RFC 4514 escaped or hex-encoded form exceeds 256 bytes overflowed that buffer, so the function returned `MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL` even when the caller's buffer was large enough.

The value is now written straight to the output buffer through the existing `MBEDTLS_X509_SAFE_SNPRINTF` path, so the error is returned only when the output buffer is really too small. Output is unchanged for DNs that already fit. Regression tests cover the escaped and hex paths.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [ ] **framework PR** not required
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto development PR** not required because: the fix is in mbedtls x509 code, no crypto change
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto 1.1 PR** not required because: the fix is in mbedtls x509 code, no crypto change
- [x] **mbedtls development PR** this PR
- [ ] **mbedtls 4.1 PR** to follow after development review
- [ ] **mbedtls 3.6 PR** to follow after development review
- [x] **tests** provided
labels: bug needs-review component-x509 needs-ci needs-reviewer size-s priority-low
title: Fix mbedtls_x509_dn_gets buffer too small for long DN values

body_excerpt:
[ This issue is a mishmash of a question, feature request and bug report. Unclear what category it fits best. Closely related to, but different from #10542 ]

Presently, when reporting the nominal bit counts of ECDSA private or public keys as part of the `"TEXT"` output format (e.g. with `openssl pkey -text ...`), the value used is the raw bit count of the order $`n`$ of the group's base point (element count of the generated cyclic group, i.e. the number of possible distinct private keys).
This is quite reasonable in the many cases in which the order is just below a power of two (when the group order is $`2^k - ε`$ the reported bit count is $`k`$).
It is at least somewhat surprising to report $`k+1`$ when the group order is $`2^k + ε`$. Yes it takes $`k+1`$ bits to represent the exact order, but what's relevant in terms of the key's security is that there are roughly $`2^k`$ possible private key values. The entropy of a uniform distribution over the private keys is essentially $`k`$, not $`k+1`$.

So perhaps we should round towards the nearest power of 2 (on a log scale), mapping $`2^k \pm ε \rightarrow k`$.

This would result in reporting 1 bit less for the curves listed below.

[ In some of the cases the group order is quite far from a 2-power, it is instead approximately $`{4/3} \cdot 2^k \pm ε`$ with a fractional entropy of  $`\log_2 (4/3) \sim 0.42`$. These are also rounded down.
If rounding the key space entropy bits to the nearest whole number is not your cup tea, what
labels: triaged: feature
title: Better rounding of ECDSA key bit counts reported with `-text` key outputs?

body_excerpt:
I create a X509 certificate with  Botan C++ API where I left the issuer field empty, and then load it in openssl for QUIC (both server and client).

This has partially worked but at some point the connection from server to client dies stops working, as described in #28468. Investigation at that time showed connection breaks when there is a flight with "handshake_done+new_token+crypto".
Also while investigating created #28678 which is a independent issue.

One important observation I have tried to load a bad certificate and replace it with a good one immediately after, before start any communications:

SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_ASN1(...bad cert key)
SSL_CTX_use_certificate_ASN1(...bad cert)
SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_ASN1(...good cert key)
SSL_CTX_use_certificate_ASN1(...good cert)

And also in the reverse order:

SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_ASN1(...good cert key)
SSL_CTX_use_certificate_ASN1(...good cert)
SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_ASN1(...bad cert key)
SSL_CTX_use_certificate_ASN1(...bad cert)

All calls always return 1 (success).
 
And in both cases the bug gets exposed so some state is getting corrupt by the empty Issuer.


To create a certificate that exposes this issue:

```
/*
Install dependencies (Debian): sudo apt install libbotan-3-dev
Compile with: g++ main.cpp --std=c++23 -I/usr/include/botan-3 -lbotan-3
*/

#include <chrono>
#include <fstream>

#include <botan/x509_ca.h>
#include <botan/x509self.h>
#include <botan/pkcs8.h>
#include <botan/pk_algs.h>
#include <botan/pubkey.h>
#inc

body_excerpt:
Impacts OpenSSL 3.0 to 3.6

I'm using OpenSSL 3.5

FYI, I note I am being pedantry with this comment, but I also note many people have made poor implementation decisions based  on the documentation language used.

The "openssl s_server" “-verifyCAfile file” directive indicates “A file in PEM format **CA** containing **trusted certificates** to use for verifying client certificates.”
Note, the term “**trusted certificates**” is interpreted by many to mean Self-Signed Root CAs, where Issuing CAs are interpreted by OpenSSL to be additional “untrusted” certificates required to create a certificate chain.

I’m wondering if there is benefit in being very explicit with this comment. E.g. “An authentication file in PEM format containing trusted Issuing CA certificates used for verifying the client's certificate. Note: if trusted Self-Signed Root CAs are included within this PEM formatted authentication file, then every certificate issued under the PKI may be trusted as a client authentication certificate.” 

Systems admins are encouraged to regularly verify the acceptable authenticator certificates for Mutual TLS by reviewing the logged data  “acceptable client certificates“ from the openssl s_client.

Regards
Nigel
labels: triaged: documentation
title: “openssl s_server” comment for “-verifyCAfile”.

I’ve been testing Mutual TLS for TLSv1.3 and looking at the “openssl s_client” command directives that allow the client to offer a client certificate chain as an alternative to just the client certificate.

The “openssl s_client” commands “-certform” ,“-cert-chain” & “-build_chain” directives aid the certificate chain to be built from untrusted certificates. However, I was surprised the chain building error unless  “-cert_chain” directive file included the "trusted" Self-Signed Root CA. 

I do understand there is an argument that a full chain cannot be built if you don’t reach a Self-Signed Root CA – However, the server's requirement to send a client the server's certificate, all Issuing CA and Intermediate Certificates, and optionally the Self-Signed Root CA doesn’t apply to the client, when referencing the standards (I do need to go find this reference). Logically, a client can send any subset of the certificate chain that it chooses, providing the server can process the client certificate chain subset.

Therefore, my question is why in the “open s_client” command is the -cert_chain” directive documentation indicating the PEM formatted file contains “untrusted certificates”, yet the inclusion of a trusted Self-Signed Root certificate is mandatory. I therefore also assume all Issuing CA & Intermediate certificates MUST be present in the file identified by the "-cert_chain" directive?

body_excerpt:
This issue follows #[27603](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/27603) concerns handling of sensitive key material (CSPs) whose lifetime ends without guaranteed zeroization, including inside the FIPS provider.

There are code paths where private keys or other sensitive buffers are allocated on the normal heap and later freed without an explicit cleanse. free() does not guarantee overwrite, so key material remains recoverable after release.

For code executing inside the FIPS boundary, this has two implications:

Compliance: FIPS 140-3 expects sensitive security parameters to be zeroized when no longer needed. Allowing CSPs to persist in freed memory complicates justification during CMVP review.

Security: Residual key material materially increases the impact of unrelated memory disclosure bugs (e.g., over-reads, UAFs). This is a known historical failure mode (Heartbleed-class issues).

This is not a proposal for new hardening functionality, but an observation that some sensitive data is not reliably cleared at end-of-life.

Context

We are preparing an upcoming patch-set that addresses multiple instances of sensitive data lifetime issues, including code paths inside the FIPS provider. This issue is intended to document the problem space and provide context for those changes, while coming to a decision on feature vs bug treatment for these issues.
labels: issue: bug report
title: Sensitive key material not consistently zeroized on free (incl. FIPS provider)

body_excerpt: Hi, I’ve found a potential null pointer dereference issue in the project and would like to report it to the maintainers. Could you please help take a look and confirm whether this is a real issue? Thanks a lot for your time and help! Below is the execution path that may lead to the null pointer dereference. In `crypto/ex_data.c`, the function `CRYPTO_get_ex_data` is designed to return `NULL` when the internal stack `ad->sk` is not initialized or when the given index is out of bounds. This return value is passed through `UI_method_get_ex_data` (defined in `crypto/ui/ui_lib.c`) and is then assigned to the pointer `data` inside the `ui_read` function in `crypto/ui/ui_util.c`. At the moment, there is no check to ensure that `data` is valid before it is used. As shown in the image, after steps 1 through 5 are executed, the variable `data` may end up being `NULL`. This can lead to an unsafe pointer dereference in `crypto/ui/ui_util.c` when the code attempts to invoke the callback via `data->cb`. Thank you very much for reading, and I look forward to your feedback!
labels: issue: bug report resolved: not a bug
title: Potential Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in crypto/ui/ui_util.c

body_excerpt:
## OpenSSL UBSan Type Mismatch in EVP_DigestUpdate

Dear OpenSSL developers,

I found an UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer error in OpenSSL when using the EVP digest API.

### Version

openssl-3.4.0-alpha1-2545-gc8b4a397d0

### Description

The crash is a UBSan function type mismatch in `EVP_DigestUpdate` at `crypto/evp/digest.c:299`. When calling `EVP_Digest()` with `EVP_sha256()`, the library dispatches through a function pointer `ctx->digest->dupdate` which has type `OSSL_FUNC_digest_update_fn*`. However, it points to `SHA256_Update` which has signature `int SHA256_Update(SHA256_CTX*, const void*, size_t)`.

The type mismatch is:
- Expected by function pointer: `int (*)(void *, const unsigned char *, unsigned long)`
- Actual function signature: `int SHA256_Update(SHA256_CTX*, const void*, size_t)`

This violates strict aliasing rules and is undefined behavior according to the C standard. The issue appears to be in the bridge between legacy digest implementations and the new provider architecture.

### Reproduction Steps

1. Compile the PoC:
```bash
clang++ -g -fsanitize=address,undefined \
    -I/path/to/openssl/include \
    poc.cpp -o poc \
    -L/path/to/openssl/lib64 -lcrypto \
    -Wl,-rpath,/path/to/openssl/lib64
```

2. Run with UBSan:
```bash
export UBSAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1"
./poc
```

### Stack Trace

```
$ export UBSAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1"
$ ./poc
crypto/evp/digest.c:299:12: runtime error: call to function SHA256_Update t
labels: triaged: feature
title: An UBSan Type Mismatch in EVP_DigestUpdate # Exp_1_000

## OpenSSL UBSan Type Mismatch in OPENSSL_sk_pop_free

Dear OpenSSL developers,

I found an UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer error when parsing malformed X.509 certificates.

### Version

openssl-3.4.0-alpha1-2545-gc8b4a397d0

### Description

The crash is a UBSan function type mismatch in `OPENSSL_sk_pop_free` at `crypto/stack/stack.c:443`. When parsing a malformed X.509 certificate with `d2i_X509()`, the cleanup path calls `X509_NAME_ENTRY_free` through a `void (*)(void *)` function pointer, but `X509_NAME_ENTRY_free` has signature `void (*)(X509_NAME_ENTRY *)`.

This type mismatch constitutes Undefined Behavior and will cause a potential runtime crash (DoS) on systems compiled with Control Flow Integrity (CFI) enabled (e.g., -fsanitize=cfi).

The vulnerable code at line 443:
```c
func((void *)st->data[i]);  // func has wrong type
```

### Reproduction Steps

1. Compile the PoC:
```bash
clang++ -g -fsanitize=address,undefined \
    -I<openssl_include> poc.cpp -o poc \
    -L<openssl_lib64> -lcrypto -Wl,-rpath,<openssl_lib64>
```

2. Run:
```bash
export UBSAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1"
./poc
```

### Stack Trace

```
$ export UBSAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1" && ./poc
crypto/stack/stack.c:443:17: runtime error: call to function X509_NAME_ENTRY_free through pointer to incorrect function type 'void (*)(void *)'
/root/src/openssl/crypto/x509/x_name.c:51: note: X509_NAME_ENTRY_free defined here
    #0 0x7bf783559e6b in OPENSSL_sk_pop_free /root/src/o
labels: branch: master triaged: feature
title: An UBSan Type Mismatch in OPENSSL_sk_pop_free # Exp_1_001

## OpenSSL UBSan Type Mismatch in ossl_bsearch (Provider Comparison)

Dear OpenSSL developers,

I found an UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer error in OpenSSL when creating SSL contexts.

### Version

openssl-3.4.0-alpha1-2545-gc8b4a397d0

### Description

The crash is a UBSan function type mismatch in `ossl_bsearch` at `crypto/bsearch.c:28`. When calling `SSL_CTX_new()`, the library internally performs a binary search for providers using `ossl_provider_find()`. The comparison function `ossl_provider_cmp` is called through a function pointer with incorrect type signature.

The bug is triggered during OpenSSL internal initialization - no specific input data is required. The call chain is:

```
SSL_CTX_new -> RAND_bytes_ex -> rand_get0_public -> rand_get0_primary
-> rand_new_drbg -> EVP_RAND_instantiate -> evp_rand_instantiate_locked
-> drbg_ctr_instantiate_wrapper -> drbg_ctr_set_ctx_params_locked
-> ossl_provider_find -> sk_OSSL_PROVIDER_find -> OPENSSL_sk_find
-> internal_find -> ossl_bsearch -> CRASH (type mismatch)
```

### Reproduction Steps

1. Compile the PoC:
```bash
clang++ -g -fsanitize=address,undefined \
    -I<openssl_include> poc.cpp -o poc \
    -L<openssl_lib64> -lssl -lcrypto \
    -Wl,-rpath,<openssl_lib64>
```

2. Run:
```bash
export UBSAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1"
./poc
```

### Stack Trace

```
$ export UBSAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1" && ./poc
crypto/bsearch.c:28:13: runtime error: call to function ossl_provider_cmp through po

body_excerpt:
The ECDSA ```KAT_Signature``` selftests performed during e.g. ```openssl fipsinstall``` are susceptible to wall-clock changes. The tests may fail if, after initialization of the KAT DRBG, the system time is adjusted either backwards by a few seconds or forwards by a sufficiently large amount (e.g. ≥ 3600 seconds).

Such a time change triggers a reseed of the KAT DRBG, which breaks the determinism required for a Known Answer Test (KAT). As a result, the ECDSA ```KAT_Signature``` selftest may fail. This issue can be reliably reproduced using the attached shell script [reproduce.sh](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24611879/reproduce.sh), preferably executed in a virtual machine.

Although this situation (a reseed of the KAT DRBG during the selftest) may appear unlikely, I have observed it multiple times on a system I maintain. In practice, it can occur during early boot when system services adjust the wall clock while ```openssl fipsinstall``` is running.

Disabling time-based reseeding for the KAT DRBG mitigates the problem and makes the selftest robust against wall-clock changes. A potential patch addressing this issue can be found here: [fix-kat-drbg.patch](https://github.com/ssapalski/openssl/commit/5dee0a606d522111f6f445e68715467757adecb6)

The reseed is triggered by the following condition in the DRBG implementation: [drbg.c](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/0893a62353583343eb712adef6debdfbe597c227/providers/implementations/rands/drbg.c#L676)

This problem
labels: branch: master triaged: bug branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: FIPS: ECDSA KAT DRBG is susceptible to wall-clock changes

body_excerpt:
OpenSSL's QUIC stack already exposes limited APIs to get/set individual QUIC transport parameters, for example `max_idle_timeout` via [`SSL_get_value_uint()` / `SSL_set_value_uint()`](https://docs.openssl.org/3.6/man3/SSL_get_value_uint/). However, for advanced client/server configuration, it would be very useful to expose additional QUIC transport parameters via a public API. There seems to be user demand for this: #29423

At the moment, most QUIC transport parameters appear to be either fixed internally or not configurable at all, which limits tuning QUIC behavior for specific environments, for example advanced applications that need explicit control over transport limits.

I propose making the following transport parameters retrievable/configurable as they are also commonly configurable in other QUIC stacks (e.g., [quiche](https://docs.quic.tech/quiche/struct.Config.html#method.set_max_idle_timeout)):
* `disable_active_migration`
* `active_connection_id_limit`
* `initial_max_data`
* `initial_max_stream_data_{uni, bidi_local, bidi_remote}`
* `initial_max_streams_{uni, bidi}`
* `max_ack_delay`
* `ack_delay_exponent`
* `max_udp_payload_size`

I would be happy to provide the necessary patches but before starting the work, I wanted to ask whether this is something the OpenSSL project would be interested in accepting upstream and if there are any design constraints or preferred directions I should follow.

Thanks.
labels: help wanted triaged: feature
title: QUIC: Make more transport parameters configurable

openssl : master

During certificate verification, when X509_V_FLAG_PARTIAL_CHAIN and X509_V_FLAG_OCSP_RESP_CHECK_ALL are set, if the OCSP check is performed on the last certificate (non-self-signed certificate), the issuer of the certificate is set to NULL, but the OCSP response may pass the verification. When the OCSP_cert_to_id function is called, an empty pointer is accessed.

check_revocation():
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/85bc702cad79a18b9e0fa8b7a9beef9d91ba4782/crypto/x509/x509_vfy.c#L1164-L1166

check_cert_ocsp_resp():
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/85bc702cad79a18b9e0fa8b7a9beef9d91ba4782/crypto/x509/x509_vfy.c#L1284-L1288

Therefore, the last certificate cannot be verified using OCSP.

Dear developers,

consider having a certificate chain of 3 CA certificates and one server certificate like this:

level-1.pem --> root CA
level-2.pem
level-3.pem
cert.pem

I would expect that as long as I add level-1.pem to the trust store the verification of cert.pem should work. But this is unfortunately not the case as you can see in this output:

```
> openssl-3 verify -trusted level-1.pem -untrusted level-2.pem -untrusted level-3.pem cert.pem
cert.pem: OK
> openssl-3 verify -trusted level-1.pem -untrusted level-2.pem -trusted level-3.pem cert.pem
CN = Level3CA
error 2 at 1 depth lookup: unable to get issuer certificate
error cert.pem: verification failed
> openssl-3 verify -trusted level-1.pem -trusted level-2.pem -trusted level-3.pem cert.pem
cert.pem: OK
> openssl-3 verify -trusted level-1.pem -trusted level-2.pem -untrusted level-3.pem cert.pem
cert.pem: OK
```

As you can see, if I add level-1 and level-3 but not level-2 to the trust store, the verification fails unexpectedly, whereas the other combinations succeed.

I'm not sure if this behavior complies with the [documentation](https://docs.openssl.org/1.0.2/man1/verify/#verify-operation) and is actually expected. There it is mentioned that

> The lookup first looks in the list of untrusted certificates and if no match is found the remaining lookups are from the trusted certificates

I was in two minds regards “is this a bug” or “is this a documentation” issue? I chose bug because it will be an easier decision to kick it to documentation, if appropriate. 

An interesting issue with OpenSSL 3.5.1 on CentOS 9.

I’m just testing some TLSv1.3 mutual authentication issues using OpenSSL **s_client** and **s_server**. Within my **s_server** config I include the “**-servername**” directive. Surprisingly, to me, the inclusion of the “**-servername**” directive triggers the need to configure and use the “**-key2**” and “**-cert2**” directives. The “**s_server**” “**-servername**” documentation doesn’t suggest this, although “**-cert2**” documentation sort of does. body_excerpt:
From the `BIO_read` man page:

```
       All other functions return either the amount of data successfully read
       or written (if the return value is positive) or that no data was
       successfully read or written if the result is 0 or -1. If the return
       value is -2 then the operation is not implemented in the specific BIO
       type.

NOTES
       A 0 or -1 return is not necessarily an indication of an error. In
       particular when the source/sink is nonblocking or of a certain type it
       may merely be an indication that no data is currently available and
       that the application should retry the operation later.
```

A return of -1 (or zero) means no data was written and:
1. it might or might not indicate an error and
2. there is no other return value for an error.
This means that there is no way of detecting an error case reliably.

This impacts `BIO_read`, `BIO_puts`, `BIO_get_line`.
labels: issue: bug report
title: Detecting BIO_read errors isn't possible

body_excerpt:
### Description
In OpenSSL 4.0, `EVP_SignFinal()` no longer respects per-key method overrides attached to asymmetric key objects. This breaks integrations that rely on documented per-key method overrides attached to asymmetric key objects. When a key is wrapped in an `EVP_PKEY`, `EVP_SignFinal()` appears to bypass the key’s attached method and instead performs the operation using the default internal implementation.

This is a behavioral regression relative to historical OpenSSL behavior.

### Expected behavior
If an EVP_PKEY contains an asymmetric key object (RSA*, EC_KEY*, etc.) with a custom per-key method attached, then:
- high-level signing APIs such as `EVP_SignFinal(`) should honor that method
- the custom callbacks (e.g. priv_enc, sign) should be invoked.

This behavior is required for backward compatibility of non-provider code.

### Actual behavior
- Low-level APIs (`RSA_sign()`, `ECDSA_sign()`) correctly invoke the custom per-key methods
- `EVP_SignFinal()` does not invoke the custom per-key methods
- instead, the default internal implementation is used.

This indicates that `EVP_SignFinal()` no longer operates on the original key object, or otherwise discards its attached method.
This strongly suggests that EVP_SignFinal() no longer performs signing operations via the underlying key object.

### Minimal reproducer (RSA)
The following minimal PoC installs a custom `RSA_METHOD` that increments a counter when the private operation is invoked.

Observed output on Fedo
labels: triaged: bug severity: regression
title: `EVP_SignFinal()` ignores documented per-key method overrides in OpenSSL 4.0.0-dev (behavioral regression)

body_excerpt:
There are two issues with SSL_sendfile() misreporting the state of the connection.

1. When the underlying socket is in non-blocking mode a short write can happen. In this case the BIO_should_retry() is not set. The only way for the application to check why the write was short is to repeat it again. If the buffer space has not been cleared, the SSL_sendfile() will return -1 and will raise BIO_should_retry(). However, on FreeBSD the sendfile(2) API allows to tell a short write due to insufficient buffer space from a short write due to end of file. Bringing this logic up to the level of SSL_sendfile() will allow applications to issue 2x less syscalls. So this issue is a performance bug.

2. SSL_sendfile() does not reset the BIO state. So, if SSL_sendfile() has raised the BIO_should_retry() flag once, and no SSL_write() was ever issued on the connection, the flag will stay there indefinitely no matter if following SSL_sendfile()s were successful.
labels: issue: bug report
title: SSL_sendfile() has issues with BIO_should_retry() raising and clearing

body_excerpt:
openssl 3.5/3.6 (introduced in 3.5, also appears on 3.6 latest commit: f99912c4a463dffa8d8fc4536d22dd34d5293cdf)

The state machine in the `OSSL_HTTP_REQ_CTX_nbio` function of the http_client is done in such way to allow reading the response's content even when receiving an error code (>=400); see openssl/openssl#25541 , exactly [this](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/25541/files#diff-aee45bfb9277b1b51782bc054923d52480c1e30270204cd8a05a5602fba16887L736) change which sets the next state after parsing the first HTTP response line:

```diff
- rctx->state = OHS_HEADERS_ERROR;
- goto next_line; /* continue parsing and reporting header */
+ /* must return content if status >= 400 */
+ rctx->state = i < HTTP_STATUS_CODES_NONFATAL_ERROR
+     ? OHS_HEADERS_ERROR : OHS_HEADERS;
+ goto next_line; /* continue parsing, also on HTTP error */
```


However when this scenario (receving 4XX or 5XX) happens with an OSSL_HTTP_REQ_CTX which has expect_asn1 (as would happen when using the new openssl3.0+ macro `X509_CRL_load_http`), the state machine will still try to parse the response as asn1 encoded content, resulting a couple of issues: 
1. If the 4XX or 5XX response has at least 2 bytes of content, you will get the following error message:  `Got bad crl because: error:1E80006E:HTTP routines::missing asn1 encoding` which is extremely misleading (this is the case I faced and it started quite a long investigation)
2. If the response content is empty you will probably get `Got bad crl be
labels: branch: master triaged: bug branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: OSSL_HTTP_REQ_CTX_nbio incorrectly handles HTTP errors when expecting asn1 encoded response.

body_excerpt:
[Commit 722c976](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/722c9762f2e021a9b43774fca282c9a4146d38e6) ("Harmonize the make variables across all known platforms families") changes the handling of LDFLAGS so that it is joined into BIN_LDFLAGS, LIB_LDFLAGS, etc...

However, while the Unix makefile template is changed in this commit to no longer use LDFLAGS directly when linking shared libs and bins, the Windows makefile template is not. Henceforth, LDFLAGS is introduced both by directly using it when linking shared libs and bins and via BIN_LDFLAGS/LIB_LDFLAGS/etc.

For example, on Windows I'm attempting to use a custom manifest to enable long path support so that OpenSSL tests pass with my current directory layout.

I'm setting the LDFLAGS environment variable similar to: `/manifest:embed /manifestinput:longpathsupport.manifest`

But in the build output, I'm seeing:
`...\link.exe /manifest:embed /manifestinput:longpathsupport.manifest /dll /manifest:embed /manifestinput:longpathsupport.manifest ...`

In my case, this leads to an invalid merged manifest being generated since the embedded manifest includes duplicated nodes from the duplicate-specified manifest file.
labels: issue: bug report
title: LDFLAGS gets duplicated in Windows builds

Openssl version 3.6.0 builds successfully (regression issue).

My main purpose is to document the issue as you may or may not perceive it as an upstream problem.

Missing  SIO_UDP_NETRESET in the Mingw64 Mswsock.h:
```
#define TCP_BSDURGENT 0x7000

#define SIO_UDP_CONNRESET _WSAIOW(IOC_VENDOR,12)

#define SIO_SOCKET_CLOSE_NOTIFY _WSAIOW(IOC_VENDOR,13)
#endif /* >= XP && < VISTA */
#if (_WIN32_WINNT >= 0x0600)
#define SIO_BSP_HANDLE _WSAIOR(IOC_WS2,27)
#define SIO_BSP_HANDLE_SELECT _WSAIOR(IOC_WS2,28)
#define SIO_BSP_HANDLE_POLL _WSAIOR(IOC_WS2,29)
```

Commenting out the following in quic_reactor.c results in successful build.
```
 if (bpd->type == BIO_POLL_DESCRIPTOR_TYPE_SOCK_FD) {
        WSAIoctl(bpd->value.fd, SIO_UDP_CONNRESET, &bNewBehavior,
            sizeof(bNewBehavior), NULL, 0, &dwBytesReturned, NULL, NULL);
     //   WSAIoctl(bpd->value.fd, SIO_UDP_NETRESET, &bNewBehavior,
     //       sizeof(bNewBehavior), NULL, 0, &dwBytesReturned, NULL, NULL);
```
Similarly adding the following to Mswsock.h results in a successful build

#define SIO_UDP_NETRESET            _WSAIOW(IOC_VENDOR,15)

#2  0x00007cc295e1ebaf in ossl_init_base_ossl_ () from /usr/pkg/lib/[libcrypto.so](http://libcrypto.so/).3
#3  0x00007cc29f407cb7 in pthread_once (once_control=0x7cc2963b28c0 <base>, routine=0x7cc295e1eb7c <ossl_init_base_ossl_>)
   at /usr/src/lib/libpthread/pthread_once.c:66
#4  0x00007cc295e2db56 in CRYPTO_THREAD_run_once () from /usr/pkg/lib/[libcrypto.so](http://libcrypto.so/).3
#5  0x00007cc295e1eda3 in OPENSSL_init_crypto () from /usr/pkg/lib/[libcrypto.so](http://libcrypto.so/).3
#6  0x00007cc296435802 in OPENSSL_init_ssl () from /usr/pkg/lib/[libssl.so](http://libssl.so/).3
```

The crash occurs in crypto/initthread.c, in the first condition:

```
int ossl_init_thread(void)
{
    if (CRYPTO_THREAD_compare_id(recursion_guard,
            CRYPTO_THREAD_get_current_id()))
        return 1;
    if (!RUN_ONCE(&ossl_init_thread_runonce, ossl_init_thread_once))
        return 0;
    return 1;
}
```

On NetBSD, pthread_self() seems to return -1 for the main thread, and unfortunately that has been chosen as guard:

```
static CRYPTO_THREAD_ID recursion_guard = (CRYPTO_THREAD_ID)-1;
```

Changing that to 0 (zero) fixes the problem for me:

```
static CRYPTO_THREAD_ID recursion_guard = (CRYPTO_T
labels: triaged: bug severity: regression branch: 3.3 branch: 3.4 branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: 3.6.1 crashes on NetBSD

body_excerpt:
Please create fips branches for:
- 3.0.9
- 3.1.2
- 3.5.4

They must start off the validated code or submitted code.

These branches will only receive updates to the in-boundary code if it fixes a CVE, such that these branches can be submitted for revalidation under the CVE security bugfix scenario.

Also these branches will then be possible to use as the update stream under the FedRAMP cryptogrpahic module selection policy.

These branches will not receive updates that are outside of the FIPS boundary, unless needed for revalidation.

The intention is that these branches are only used for split builds => specifically such that one uses these branches to build the fips.so provider, and the libcrypto/libssl/openssl tools all otherwise come from any of the other stable brances to receive cve fixes and features that are all outside of the boundary.

In the advisories notifications, explicitly state if these branches are affected in-boundary. For example the recent January CVE updates didn't mention if the 3.0.9; 3.1.2; 3.5.4 FIPS providers were not affected. But it would be useful to state so. Also it would be useful to update https://openssl-library.org/news/fips-cve/ with the january cves with explicit reference to the 3.0.9; 3.1.2; 3.5.4.

This practice is similar to what all other fips maintainers do:
- boringssl has fips- branches, which receive validation related fixes only (test code updates, or changes requested by NIST, without any other in-boundary updates)
- aws-lc has
labels: issue: feature request
title: Please create fips branches with different support policies

body_excerpt:
Many compliance frameworks have additional levels that restrict algorithm choices to a smaller set of algorithms; and/or higher minimum acceptable keys; that is allowed by existing FIPS certificates; new FIPS submissions; or future FIPS drafts.

It would be helpful to be able to support such mode in OpenSSL both default provider, and inside the fips provider.

Boringssl has "cryptogrpahic policy" features which with a single flag is able to opt into FIPS-2022/05 mode or the CNSA mode. See https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/aa202db1d7091b88b80f0a58c630c5c1aefc817d/include/openssl/ssl.h#L6057

These changed in a single call allowed ciphersuites; allowed algorithms for certificate validation; and key sizes.

Today fips provider / libcrypto / libssl don't have good settings but it would be nice to be able to do that.

Possibly, this needs to be done in the providers; but it would be nice to have this sort on the core api - for example filter the algorithms returned by the providers.

Specifically what is desired is to have ability to opt into the CNSA 2.0. https://www.nsa.gov/Press-Room/Digital-Media-Center/Document-Gallery/igphoto/2003071836/

https://media.defense.gov/2022/Sep/07/2003071836/-1/-1/0/CSI_CNSA_2.0_FAQ_.PDF

It would be interesting, if it is possible to block RSA, ECDSA to support CNSA.

I don't know if this can be like the SSL_OP thing (which is nice for higher level apps to elect, like the preffer CHACHA SSL_OP). Possibly on the core provider fetch apis tha
labels: triaged: feature
title: Additional cryptographic policies to support more strict fips levels

When using openssl 3.6.1 on a ppc64 big endian/ELFv2 configuration (on POWER9 machine, Chimera Linux), I get unknown TLS errors from all sorts of things (package manager, rust/cargo, etc.). Previous commit works fine.

https://gist.github.com/q66/f7aedc47809ab93d4f38a75ef37c51ea

diff --git a/include/crypto/aes_platform.h b/include/crypto/aes_platform.h

+++ b/include/crypto/aes_platform.h

 #define HWAES_xts_decrypt aes_p8_xts_decrypt
 #endif /* OPENSSL_SYS_MACOSX */

+#if defined(__BIG_ENDIAN__)
+#define PPC_AES_GCM_CAPABLE 0
+#else
 #define PPC_AES_GCM_CAPABLE (OPENSSL_ppccap_P & PPC_MADD300)
+#endif
 #define AES_GCM_ENC_BYTES 128
 #define AES_GCM_DEC_BYTES 128

```

body_excerpt:
## Description

I have identified a logic flaw in the `bn_mul_mont_adx` assembly implementation on x86_64, specifically in the Montgomery multiplication path using ADX/BMI2 instructions.

Under specific boundary conditions, the synchronization between the parallel carry chains (`ADCX` / `ADOX`) can fail, resulting in a deterministic 1-bit error in the final result.

This issue is **specific to the ADX/BMI2 assembly path** and does **not** affect:
- the generic C implementation, or
- the non-ADX (standard ADC) assembly path.

---

## Technical Analysis

### Root Cause

A carry synchronization issue occurs at the merge point of the parallel carry chains used by ADX instructions.

In the `bn_mul_mont_adx` loop, a carry generated by the `ADOX` chain can be dropped before it is fully integrated into the higher-order limb. This happens at a limb boundary when a specific all-ones word pattern coincides with a carry-in from the previous limb.

Conceptually, this manifests as a “phase shift” between the two carry chains (CF and OF), where one carry is consumed or overwritten before the merge is complete.

### Verification Context

- **C implementation / Non-ADX path**  
  Stable. No incorrect results observed.  
  (Confirmed by disabling ADX via `OPENSSL_ia32cap="~0x80000"`.)

- **ADX/BMI2 assembly path**  
  Incorrect result observed under the boundary conditions described below.  
  (Confirmed with `OPENSSL_ia32cap="0x80000"`.)

### Suspected Location

The issue appears to occur in
title: Potential 1-bit carry loss in bn_mul_mont_adx (x86_64 ADX/BMI2 Montgomery multiplication)

Environment:
OpenSSL Version: 3.5.5
Platforms: Solaris 10/11 (32-bit SPARC, 32-bit x86, 64-bit x86_64)
Compiler: Sun Studio (cc/CC)

Problem:
1) All SSL/TLS connections fail due to hashtable corruption in the new hashtable implementation.
2) And there are no mac-algorithms lists.

Root Cause:
The hashtable structure ht_neighborhood_entry_st contains a uint64_t hash field followed by a pointer. On solaris platforms atomic_or_64_nv() is causing issue and memory is getting corrupted something like below:
32-bit Solaris (SPARC/AMD)
[HT_INSERT] key=key0 hash=2a7824d1ecaf6218
[HT_GET] key=key0 search_hash=2a7824d1ecaf6218
[HT_CMP] stored=000000002a7824d1 search=2a7824d1ecaf6218

struct ht_neighborhood_entry_st in hashtable.c has 12-byte size on 32-bit platforms (uint64_t hash + pointer)
Solaris atomic_swap_64() requires 8-byte natural alignment but silently corrupts data when misaligned
Hash corruption observed: 0x2a7824d1ecaf6218 → 0x000000002a7824d1 (SPARC) or 0xffffffffecaf6218 (AMD x86)

Temporary Workaround:
Disabled atomic_or_64_nv in threads_pthread.c line 847, forcing mutex-protected fallback.
int CRYPTO_atomic_load(uint64_t *val, uint64_t *ret, CRYPTO_RWLOCK *lock) {
#if defined(__GNUC__) && defined(__ATOMIC_ACQ_REL) && !defined(BROKEN_CLANG_ATOMICS)
    if (__atomic_is_lock_free(sizeof(*val), val)) {
        __atomic_load(val, ret, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE);
        return 1;
    }
**#elif 0 && defined(__sun) && (defined(__SunOS_5_10) || defined(__SunOS_5_11))**
    /* This will
labels: issue: bug report

body_excerpt:
Platform:
  Linux IA-64 (Itanium)
  Kernel: 2.6.18-194.el5
  Architecture: ia64

OpenSSL version:
OpenSSL 3.5.5

Observed behavior:
  Compilation fails in bss_dgram.c:

    bss_dgram.c: In function `dgram_ctrl':
    bss_dgram.c:965: error: `IPV6_PMTUDISC_PROBE' undeclared (first use in this function)
    bss_dgram.c:965: error: (Each undeclared identifier is reported only once
    bss_dgram.c:965: error: for each function it appears in.)
    make[2]: *** [bss_dgram.o] Error 1

Expected behavior:
  Build should complete successfully without errors.

Additional information:
  - This is specific to IA-64 (Itanium) platforms.
  - Kernel 2.6.18 is too old to define IPV6_PMTUDISC_PROBE.
  -The build fails because IPV6_PMTUDISC_PROBE is used without checking for its presence.

A safe fix is to conditionally compile the code using IPV6_PMTUDISC_PROBE only when it is defined, similar to how IP_PMTUDISC_PROBE is handled. I am willing to submit a patch implementing this fix.
labels: issue: bug report
title: Build failure on Linux IA-64: IPV6_PMTUDISC_PROBE undeclared in bss_dgram.c

body_excerpt:
I’ve run into an issue with OSSL_PARAM_print_to_bio() in params_from_text.c. The function definition is not wrapped in #ifndef FIPS_MODULE, even though all of its call sites are correctly excluded from FIPS builds.

**Problem**

When building fips.so dynamically by directly linking all FIPS object files (i.e., without first creating libfips.a), the build fails with unresolved symbols:
BIO_printf
BIO_dump

Although the calls to OSSL_PARAM_print_to_bio() in provider_core.c (ossl_provider_get_params and ossl_provider_gettable_params) are properly guarded, the function itself still compiles into libfips-lib-params_from_text.o. That object file is then pulled into the FIPS provider build.
Since no BIO-related sources are included in the FIPS module, this results in undefined symbol errors at link time.

**Question**

Given that all callers are already excluded from FIPS builds, should the definition of OSSL_PARAM_print_to_bio() also be wrapped in #ifndef FIPS_MODULE?
Is there a specific reason this debugging/utility function needs to exist (albeit unused) within the FIPS boundary?
labels: triaged: bug
title: Inconsistency: OSSL_PARAM_print_to_bio should be excluded from FIPS_MODULE

body_excerpt:
Current S/MIME utility even on OpenSSL 3.5.4 uses old PKCS#7 code for parsing the CMS SignedData structure. 

For example the definition of `SignedData` has contained `RevocationInfoChoices` since 2004 (RFC 3852).

However the `smime` utility (from OSSL 3.5.4) expects X509_CRL, which can result in incorrect errors like this:
```
$ openssl smime -verify -inform DER -in test.p7m
Error reading S/MIME message
A09CDF92FFFF0000:error:068000A8:asn1 encoding routines:asn1_check_tlen:wrong tag:../crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:1194:
A09CDF92FFFF0000:error:0688010A:asn1 encoding routines:asn1_item_embed_d2i:nested asn1 error:../crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:349:Type=X509_CRL
