body_excerpt:
# Design gap: multiple TLS 1.3 signature schemes sharing the same key type OID

## Summary

When implementing provider-based TLS 1.3 signature algorithms (specifically for GOST per [RFC 9367](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9367)), we hit a fundamental incompatibility between three facts.

In OpenSSL, a key type NID and a provider `keymgmt` algorithm name are effectively the same identity - `ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey()` resolves the key via `EVP_PKEY_is_a(pk, OBJ_nid2sn(nid))`, and `a_verify.c` checks the key via `EVP_PKEY_is_a(pkey, OBJ_nid2sn(pknid))`. Both paths collapse to the same `keymgmt` name. With that in mind:

1. **TLS 1.3 layer** assumes a **1:1 mapping** between `keymgmt` algorithm (key type NID) and signature scheme - one cert slot per key type
2. **X.509/CMS/CSR layer** resolves signature algorithms via **OID => NID => `keymgmt` name**, expecting each signature OID to map to a unique key type name

These constraints conflict with RFC 9367, which defines multiple TLS 1.3 code points sharing the same key type OID - and therefore the same keymgmt algorithm name.

---

## Fact 1: TLS 1.3 - one cert slot per key type

The `SSL_CTX` internally maintains a `CERT` structure with a dynamically-sized `pkeys[]` array. Slots `0..SSL_PKEY_NUM-1` (9 built-in) are for static key types (RSA, ECDSA, GOST, Ed25519, etc.), and slots `SSL_PKEY_NUM + i` are allocated for each provider-registered sigalg. In `ssl_setup_sigalgs()` ([ssl/t1_lib.c](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blo
labels: issue: bug report triaged: bug
title: Design gap: multiple TLS 1.3 signature schemes sharing the same key type OID

### Summary

`EVP_MAC` POLY1305 handles some NULL-key initialization paths inconsistently.

In OpenSSL 3.5.6, the following initialization paths crash with `SIGSEGV`:

- `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, NULL)`
- `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 1, NULL)`
- `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, end-only params)`

In the same matrix, other invalid key cases fail cleanly with `invalid key length` instead of crashing:

- non-NULL pointer with length `0`
- `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY` set to a non-NULL pointer with length `0`, `1`, `31`, or `33`

A valid 32-byte key succeeds in both the direct-key and parameter-based paths.

This indicates that the crash is specific to NULL-key initialization paths, not to POLY1305 key-length validation in general.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_poly1305_init_matrix_repro.cpp`

[openssl_poly1305_init_matrix_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28393777/openssl_poly1305_init_matrix_repro.cpp)

The reproducer runs each test case in a child process so that a crash in one case does not stop the full matrix.

The tested cases include:

- direct key argument with `NULL` and length `0`
- direct key argument with `NULL` and length `1`
- direct key argument with a non-NULL dummy pointer and length `0`
- direct key argument with a valid 32-byte key
- no direct key with no params
- no direct key with end-only params
- `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY` with `NULL,0`
- `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY` with a valid 32-byte key

### Observed behavior

title: EVP_MAC POLY1305 crashes on NULL direct-key initialization in some code paths

body_excerpt:
Whether this is a bug or a feature at this point is unclear, but I thought I'd file a bug, so that it can at least be an intentional choice one way or another. (We haven't yet evaluated callers for BoringSSL.)

`BIO_write` on a `BIO` backed by `FILE*` calls `fwrite` to write 1 object of size N instead of N objects of size 1. This means that `fwrite` will return:

* 1 if all N bytes were written
* 0 if < N bytes were written

In the (very unusual!) case that the write was partial, the caller won't know that there was a truncated write. That's inconsistent with every other `BIO`.

The main risk with fixing this is if some caller:

* Assumes a successful `BIO_write` wrote everything, and didn't carefully check the return value against the input length, just if it was negative.
* Only used `FILE*`-backed BIOs, so the above assumption is OK
* Was OK discarding information about partial writes because:
  * It's *highly* unusual, so treating it as a failure case is fine
  * It considered the file state a lost cause anyway, so it didn't care too much about preserving the partial write information

This behavior has been in OpenSSL since the oldest available commit. This comment suggests it was to work around some particularly bad stdio implementation back in the day, but it had some other side effects.

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/openssl-4.0.0/crypto/bio/bss_file.c#L169-L180
labels: branch: master triaged: bug
title: BIO_write on file BIOs does not report partial writes

body_excerpt:
**Description:**
There is a discrepancy between the OpenSSL documentation and the internal implementation of `EVP_CipherInit_ex` regarding the `enc` parameter. 

The official manual explicitly states that `enc` allows only the values `1`, `0`, or `-1`. However, dynamic analysis of some applications (specifically pre-built Nginx environments) reveals that an `enc` value of `2` is being passed to `EVP_CipherInit_ex`. 

Libcrypto successfully interprets and processes this value without throwing an error because of permissive internal implementation checks that use greater-than/lesser-than comparisons rather than strict equality checks.

**Expected Behavior:**
The implementation should either strictly reject undocumented values (like `2`) to prevent applications from relying on undefined behavior, **or the documentation should be updated to clarify how values greater than `1` are handled internally**.

**Observed Environment:**
This permissive handling was observed when analyzing the pre-built `nginx:1.27.4` docker image (ID `97662d24417b`) communicating with Libcrypto.

**Related Issue**
https://github.com/nginx/docker-nginx/issues/1050
labels: issue: bug report triaged: documentation
title: Documentation vs. Implementation conflict: EVP_CipherInit_ex tolerates 'enc' parameter value of 2

body_excerpt:
Summary

The torture_rw_low test introduced in OpenSSL 3.3.0 fails reliably on AIX (both 32-bit and 64-bit) because AIX's pthread_rwlock_t is reader-preferring (POSIX-conformant), causing the two writer threads to starve for the entire ~4-second test window.

Symptom

 test_threads: torture_rw_low: Threads did not iterate

Root cause

torture_rw_low creates two reader threads that cycle tightly acquiring/releasing a read lock and two writer threads attempting write locks. On AIX, pthread_rwlock_t gives readers priority, so the writers never acquire the lock while readers are active.

pthread_rwlockattr_setkind_np (the Linux glibc mechanism to prefer writers) is not available on AIX, so there is no portable workaround short of excluding AIX from USE_RWLOCK.

Affected platforms

 - AIX 7.x / Power (32-bit and 64-bit)

Suggested fix

In crypto/threads_pthread.c, add !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_AIX) to the USE_RWLOCK guard, alongside the existing NonStop and RISC-V exclusions:

 #if !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_NONSTOP_KERNEL) && !defined(__riscv) && !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_AIX)
 # define USE_RWLOCK
 #endif

Prior art

OpenSSL already excludes NonStop (OPENSSL_SYS_NONSTOP_KERNEL) and RISC-V (__riscv) from USE_RWLOCK for platform-specific threading reasons. AIX deserves the same treatment.
labels: issue: bug report triaged: bug
title: torture_rw_low test fails on AIX: reader-preferring rwlock starves writers

# `X509_check_host()` matches IP address against dNSName SAN — RFC 9525 violation

## Summary

`X509_check_host()` performs a plain string match between the caller-supplied name and dNSName SAN entries, without validating that the dNSName content is a valid DNS name. When a certificate contains an IP address (e.g. `127.0.0.1`) encoded as a dNSName SAN entry instead of the correct iPAddress SAN entry, `X509_check_host()` incorrectly reports a match.

Per RFC 9525 Section 3:

> dNSName MUST NOT contain an IP address.

Other implementations (Go `crypto/x509`, BoringSSL) correctly reject this. This is a spec compliance issue, not a directly exploitable vulnerability.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Generate a 3-tier certificate chain where the leaf has `DNS:127.0.0.1` in SAN (instead of `IP:127.0.0.1`)
2. Call `X509_check_host(leaf_cert, "127.0.0.1", -1, 0, NULL)`

**Expected:** Match fails — the name is an IP address and should only match iPAddress SAN entries, not dNSName.

**Actual:** Match succeeds — `X509_check_host()` performs a string comparison against the dNSName value without checking whether it contains an IP address.

PoC certificate chain (leaf cert excerpt):

```
Subject: CN = 127.0.0.1, O = GrayCert-127.0.0.1
X509v3 extensions:
    X509v3 Extended Key Usage:
        TLS Web Server Authentication
    X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
        DNS:127.0.0.1
```

Full PEM chain attached below.

## Analysis

### Affected Code Path

`crypto/x509/v3_utl.c`, function `do_x509_ch
labels: resolved: wont fix triaged: bug waiting-for: contributor response
title: `X509_check_host()` matches IP address against dNSName SAN — RFC 9525 violation

### Description

`nc_uri()` in `crypto/x509/v3_ncons.c` compares a URI subjectAltName against a permitted URI name constraint *after* copying the SAN into a C string, so an embedded NUL byte truncates the host before the comparison. A sub-CA constrained to URIs under `allowed.example` can issue a leaf whose SAN URI is `http://allowed.example\0.blocked.example/path` and OpenSSL accepts it — a name-constraint enforcement bypass.

This is the classic embedded-NUL certificate trick (CVE-2009-2408 era) resurfacing in the URI name-constraint path. The other matchers were hardened against interior NULs long ago — `cn2dnsid()` and the email matcher both reject them with `memchr()` — but the `nc_uri()` rewrite in 3.5.0 reintroduced a `strlen`-on-a-C-string pattern with no such guard.

### The code

In `nc_uri()`:

```c
uri_copy = OPENSSL_strndup((const char *)uri->data, uri->length);
OSSL_parse_url(uri_copy, &scheme, NULL, &host, ...);
...
hostlen = (int)strlen(host);
... ia5ncasecmp(host, baseptr, hostlen) ...
```

The SAN URI is a length-delimited IA5String. `OPENSSL_strndup` stops at the first NUL, so for `http://allowed.example\0.blocked.example/path` the copy becomes `http://allowed.example`, `OSSL_parse_url` returns host `allowed.example`, and the constraint comparison matches. The `.blocked.example` portion after the NUL is never seen by the check, yet it is still present in the certificate that relying parties read. There is no `memchr(uri->data, 0, uri->length)` guard, unlike
labels: triaged: bug
title: URI name-constraint bypass via embedded NUL byte (nc_uri, v3_ncons.c)

body_excerpt:
## Summary

TLS 1.3 early data can be accepted when the negotiated ciphersuite differs from the ciphersuite associated with the selected PSK, as long as both ciphersuites use the same hash.

Example:

- PSK/session cipher: `TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256`
- Negotiated cipher: `TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256`

or the reverse.

Both use SHA-256, so the current PSK compatibility check accepts the PSK. The server then accepts 0-RTT, but the CCM and CCM_8 variants use different authentication tag lengths. This leads to early-data decryption failure and a fatal `bad_record_mac` alert.

## Expected behavior

If a TLS 1.3 server selects a ciphersuite different from the ciphersuite associated with the selected PSK, it should reject early data and continue the 1-RTT PSK handshake where possible. Regular TLS 1.3 PSK resumption may still allow same-hash ciphersuite changes; the stricter requirement applies to 0-RTT.

## Actual behavior
`s->s3.tmp.new_cipher`.

This is observable with the CCM/CCM_8 pair:

- `TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256`
- `TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256`

The handshake negotiates successfully enough to accept early data, then fails with `bad_record_mac` while reading the 0-RTT records.

## Impact

This appears to be TLS 1.3 0-RTT spec non-compliance and an interoperability / denial-of-service issue.

## Reproducer

I reproduced it on current upstream master:

```text
d099e33e57 aes_wrap: prevent crash on update without a key
```

Generate a temporary certificate:

```
openssl req -x509 -newkey rs
labels: issue: bug report triaged: bug
title: TLS 1.3 early_data accepted with mismatched PSK/negotiated ciphersuite, causing CCM/CCM8 bad_record_mac

body_excerpt:
## Summary

**OpenSSL has confirmed by email that this issue will be handled as a regular bug, so I am now disclosing it publicly and submitting it to the OpenSSL public GitHub repository as requested.**


In released OpenSSL versions that support `s_client -starttls sieve`, a malicious Sieve server can reply to `STARTTLS` with exactly two non-NUL bytes and make `openssl s_client -starttls sieve` treat a non-NUL-terminated heap buffer as a C string.

A malicious server can remotely crash the client. Because the loop writes back transformed bytes while scanning, this is a heap out-of-bounds read with possible out-of-bounds writes before a `'\0'` is found.

## Details

This affects the documented `s_client -starttls sieve` path:

- `README.md` describes `openssl` as a supported command-line tool:

```text
- **openssl**
  the OpenSSL command line tool, a swiss army knife for cryptographic tasks,
  testing and analyzing.
```

- `doc/man1/openssl-s_client.pod.in` explicitly documents `-starttls sieve`:

```text
=item B<-starttls> I<protocol>
...
supported keywords are "smtp", "pop3", "imap", "ftp", "xmpp", "xmpp-server",
"irc", "postgres", "mysql", "lmtp", "nntp", "sieve" and "ldap".
```

The relevant code in the current tree is:

- `apps/s_client.c`
- `apps/lib/apps.c`

The vulnerable sequence is:

```c
mbuf_len = BIO_read(sbio, mbuf, BUFSIZZ - 1);
mbuf[mbuf_len] = '\0';
...
strncpy(sbuf, mbuf, 2);
make_uppercase(sbuf);
```

`make_uppercase()` assumes the input is NUL-terminated:
labels: issue: bug report triaged: bug
title: Remote heap out-of-bounds read/write in `openssl s_client -starttls sieve` STARTTLS response parsing

body_excerpt:
In our performance tests of OpenSSL 3.5.6, we found that compared to OpenSSL 3.0.12, the throughput of Nginx short connections dropped by about 10%.

nginx version: nginx/1.22.1

The test steps are as follows:
```
ethtool -C ${interface} adaptive-rx off
nginx -c ${NGINX_PATH}/nginx_4.conf
./wrk-4.2.0/wrk -c 2000 -d 60s -t 20 -H Connection: close --latency --timeout 5s 
```

We discovered it through binary search:
|Commit|Headline||
|-|-|-|
|e2d5742b14|Transfer the functionality from ssl3_read_n to the new record layer|-13%|
|6366bdd9be|Remove the separation betweeen enc_read_ctx and enc_write_ctx|-3%|
|2b71b04220|Create the write record layer method and object and use it|-6%|
|2b71b04220|Create the write record layer method and object and use it|-15%|

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/18132
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/19198

This part of the patch seems to be related to the record layer. May I ask if the community has noticed any degradation in this part?
labels: issue: question
title: The TLS record layer reconstruction in OpenSSL has led to a performance decline in Nginx

body_excerpt:
The ICC intel compiler is deprecated and replaced by the ICX (clang based) compiler.
Some users/companies allow critical CVS's fixes to be included into the Fips module.
Building the Fips library is done by using the latests of the OpenSSL branch version in which the Fips module was verified.
OpenSSL 3.5 => Fips 140-3
OpenSSL 3.0 => Fips 140-2
Without PR 30313 being merged into 3.0 and 3.5, the Fips modules would face a significant performance drop for users they have to change to the ICX compiler (due to not using optimised ASM).

This issue is requesting the PR 30313 to be merged into OpenSSL 3.0 and OpenSSL 3.5 branch.
title: Request PR 30313 (Add icx compiler version support in perl asm scripts) to be merged in branches they are used for Fips validation

body_excerpt:
OpenSSL's build file (Makefile) handles library directories via two
variables, 'LIBDIR' and 'libdir', where the former is empty when the
path given through ./Configure's '--libdir' is absolute.

This was forgotten when treating the resulting values in,
util/mkinstallvars.pl, which got libdir in exporters/libcrypto.pc
to not be quite right if .Configure was called with a '--libdir'
with an absolute path.

The fix turns out to be quite easy.

Resolves: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/28779
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: bug tests: deferred branch: 3.3 branch: 3.4 branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: Fix util/mkinstallvars.pl to treat LIBDIR and libdir correctly

There is a memory leak of the addrinfo struct when `./openssl s_server -dtls -sctp -accept 127.0.0.1:4433` is used, but `sysctl -w net.sctp.auth_enable=1`
is not done before.
Additionally this fixes an oversight, when
`./openssl s_client -dtls -sctp -connect localhost:4433` is used to connect to above server.
The first connect attempt is to IPv6 ::1, which might fail, but the second attempt might still succeed, so continue to try all addesses even when the SCTP socket fails for one of them.
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge branch: 3.0 branch: 3.3 branch: 3.4 branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: Fix a memory leak in sctp code

body_excerpt:
Somewhere in our conversion from .c files to .inc files for our rand providers, we created code in drbg_hmac_set_ctx_params_locked to fetch our digest and hmac when creating the rand instance.  However, the function drbg_fetch_algs_from_prov only fetched our digest for this rand type, not the hmac, and returned 1 while doing so, indicating success. This is problematic because it means that we never wind up fetching an HMAC for this rand type.  As a result we never compute the strength of the DRBG and so any attempt to seed it fails.

Ensure that, if we load a digest for this DRBG, we also fetch an HMAC, and fail if we can't do so, so the HMAC-DRBG is useful.

Fixes openssl/private#853
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge severity: fips change
title: fetch macctx while fetching digest when creating HMAC-DRBG

body_excerpt:
When using 'openssl speed' with algorithm name aliases (e.g., MLKEM512 instead of ML-KEM-512), the lookup fails even though 'openssl list' shows these as valid names.

This adds alias resolution via EVP_KEM_fetch/EVP_SIGNATURE_fetch as a fallback when exact string match fails, resolving to the canonical name for comparison.

Fixes #29355
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: bug
title: Fix algorithm name aliases in speed kem/sig lookup

body_excerpt:
Adds a fuzzer for PKCS12 parsing to improve coverage of `crypto/pkcs12/`.

The fuzzer exercises:
- `d2i_PKCS12_bio()` - DER decoding
- `PKCS12_verify_mac()` - MAC verification with NULL and empty passwords
- `PKCS12_parse()` - Full parsing with both NULL and empty password paths

This code path was previously only indirectly fuzzed through the ASN.1 fuzzer which tests structure decoding but not the higher-level parsing logic in `p12_kiss.c`.

Motivated by https://github.blog/security/vulnerability-research/bugs-that-survive-the-heat-of-continuous-fuzzing/ - PKCS12 parsing has known issues like #28615 (stack exhaustion from recursive `parse_bag()` calls) that benefit from dedicated fuzzing coverage.

Test recipe following existing patterns.
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge
title: Add PKCS12 fuzzer

body_excerpt:
The KEX Mode tests were disabled. The reason being is with PQ algorithms DTLS1.3 will fragment the packet. The fragmented client hello's two fragmented packets will be combined into something larger than the MTU size. Thus, the server never read the client hello. The client continuously sent out a new client hello thus hanging the tests.

Fixes: openssl/project#1789

body_excerpt:
Fixes #27905
When a DTLS send returns EMSGSIZE, the code previously unconditionally retried after querying the MTU. This retry is only meaningful if the MTU actually changed. The patch records the old MTU and only retries when it differs.
Verification: built locally and ran test/bad_dtls_test — ok 1 - test_bad_dtls
Please review.
title: dtls: only retry EMSGSIZE send after MTU change (fix #27905)

body_excerpt:
Optimized MLDSA NTT implementation for ppc64le (p8+).

Supporting files include,
ppc64/mldsa_ntt_ppc_4x.S: supports ossl_ml_dsa_poly_ntt. ppc64/mldsa_intt_ppc_4x.S: supports ossl_ml_dsa_poly_ntt_inverse. ppc64/mldsa_poly_ntt_mul.S: supports ossl_poly_ntt_mult. ppc64/mldsa_poly_add.S: supports poly_add and poly_sub.

Modified build.info to support ppc64le assembly implementation. Added new definitions of MLDSA_NTT_ASM and MLDSA_POLY_ADD_ASM for NTT, inverse NTT and poly_add and poly_sub for optimized assembly implementation.

This is the initial archtecture specific implementation so can be mdified to adapt to a new build structures.

Baseline speed test:
```
                       keygen     signs    verify keygens/s    sign/s  verify/s
          ML-DSA-44 0.000141s 0.000932s 0.000171s    7115.2    1073.3    5860.9
          ML-DSA-65 0.000238s 0.001471s 0.000255s    4207.2     679.6    3920.2
          ML-DSA-87 0.000340s 0.001719s 0.000387s    2939.4     581.8    2584.8
```
Optimized:
```
          ML-DSA-44 0.000072s 0.000253s 0.000060s   13866.8    3954.7   16735.7
          ML-DSA-65 0.000138s 0.000400s 0.000095s    7253.2    2500.2   10532.4
          ML-DSA-87 0.000187s 0.000485s 0.000159s    5338.5    2061.4    6269.8
```
The optimized code runs around 3.6 times faster than the original C implementation.

<!--
Thank you for your pull request. Please review these requirements:

Contributors guide: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/maste
labels: branch: master approval: review pending severity: fips change triaged: performance
title: ppc64le: Optimized MLDSA NTT, supports p8 and above architectures.

body_excerpt:
This adds the functionality to VERIFY_PARAM to separately add multiple ip's and email addresses for verification purposes.

We then mark the unfortunate SSL_add1_host API which unfortunately aquired a confusing "maybe add an IP address" behaviour as deprecated.

We replace this with SSL_set1_<dnsname, email, ip, ip_asc> and SSL_add1_<dnsname, email, ip, ip_asc> to set the things in the SSL corresponding to the VERIFY_PARAM funcitons.

Fixes: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/28418

body_excerpt:
Another trivia:

These flags seems not to be part of -Wextra, but looks like could be useful in CI. According to gcc man page:

 disabled-optimization
  Warn if a requested optimization pass is disabled.

 pointer-arith
  Warn about anything that depends on the "size of" a function type or of "void".

Fixes: https://github.com/openssl/project/issues/1809

p.s.
note to myself: to check what is in -Wall and -Wextra for gcc, use ``gcc -Wall -Wextra -Q --help=warnings``
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: feature tests: exempted
title: Add disabled-optimization and pointer-arith to compiler warnings

body_excerpt:
The DTLS1.3 ACK test is flaky. Usually when you run it the client sends the "test" data before processing the Server's NewSessionTicket. Though in scenarios when the client process the NewSessionTicket and send the ACK for it before sending the "test" data we will end up exiting the test before seeing the second NewSessionTicket and the corresponding ACK.

I updated the code to check for the two NewSessionTicket ACKs to wait for two unique NewSessionTicket ACK messages. I believe what was happening is the one ACK message was being counted twice thus breaking the test.

Fixes: openssl/project#1806

body_excerpt:
Add a compare thunk function to the STACK of macros
    
Now that ossl_bsearch is capable of using a thunking function, lets
    create a thunking function to use for the STACK_OF macros.
    
The problem we're addressing is one that gives rise to ubsan issues.
    clang-16 forward have a ubsan test that confirms that the target symbol
    that we call through a pointer matches the type of the pointer itself.
    for instance
   
``` 
    int foo(void *a, void *b)
    {
       ...
    }
    
    int (*fooptr)(char *ac, int *bc) = foo;
    
    fooptr(&charval, &intval);
    
```
is strictly speaking in C undefined behavior (even though in normal
    operation this works as expected).  Newer compilers are strict about
    this however, as several security frameworks operate with an expectation
    that this constraint is met.
    See https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/22896#issuecomment-1837266357
    for details.
    
So we need to create a thunking function.  The sole purpose of this
    thunking function is to accept the "real" comparison function for the
STACK_OF macros, along with the two items to compare of the type that
    they are passed as from the calling function, and do the convervsion of
    both the comparison function and the data pointers to the types that the
    real comparison function expects
    
So we:
    1) Modify the DEFINE_STACK_OF macros to create this thunking function
    2) Add an OPENSSL_sk_set_cmp_thu
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: feature severity: fips change tests: exempted severity: ABI change
title: add thunking for compare function to OPENSSL_STACK

body_excerpt:
The following issue was found in a memory sanitizer build of ClickHouse (which uses OpenSSL 3.5.4, from this [commit](https://github.com/ClickHouse/openssl/tree/ClickHouse/openssl-3.5.4)).

The affected method is `ossl_epki2pki_der_decode` in `decode_epki2pki.c`.
We seem to initialize less bytes in `PKCS12_pbe_crypt_ex` (by `EVP_CipherUpdate` and `EVP_CipherUpdate_ex`) than later accessed by `d2i_PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO`, leading to use of an uninitialized value. 

```
==1155903==WARNING: MemorySanitizer: use-of-uninitialized-value
    #0 0x5571e03fe712 in ASN1_get_object cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/crypto/asn1/asn1_lib.c:62:11
    #1 0x5571e0408981 in asn1_check_tlen cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:1164:13
    #2 0x5571e04048c8 in asn1_item_embed_d2i cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:346:15
    #3 0x5571e04043ba in asn1_item_ex_d2i_intern cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:118:10
    #4 0x5571e04043ba in ASN1_item_d2i_ex cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:144:9
    #5 0x5571e04043ba in ASN1_item_d2i cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/crypto/asn1/tasn_dec.c:154:12
    #6 0x5571e08460ad in ossl_epki2pki_der_decode cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/providers/implementations/encode_decode/decode_epki2pki.c:161:13
    #7 0x5571e084c5a3 in pem2der_decode cmake-build-release-msan/./contrib/openssl/providers/impleme

body_excerpt:
When `EVP_PKEY_sign_init_ex2()` is called with a pre-fetched signature algorithm that is incompatible with the key type (e.g., ECDSA signature with an RSA key), the function leaks the `tmp_keymgmt` object due to early returns that bypass cleanup.

In `evp_pkey_signature_init()`, when a pre-fetched `EVP_SIGNATURE` is provided:

1. A `tmp_keymgmt` is fetched via `evp_keymgmt_fetch_from_prov()` and the key is exported to the provider
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/84ee443446116b25030b23c0a0b5b1fe5479d987/crypto/evp/signature.c#L617-L622
2. If the signature/key type compatibility check fails, the function returns -2 directly at lines 644 or 670
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/84ee443446116b25030b23c0a0b5b1fe5479d987/crypto/evp/signature.c#L642-L645
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/84ee443446116b25030b23c0a0b5b1fe5479d987/crypto/evp/signature.c#L668-L671
3. If `EVP_SIGNATURE_up_ref()` fails, the function returns 0 directly at line 675
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/84ee443446116b25030b23c0a0b5b1fe5479d987/crypto/evp/signature.c#L674-L675

All three of these early returns bypass the cleanup labels (`end:` and `err:`) where `EVP_KEYMGMT_free(tmp_keymgmt)` is called, causing the keymgmt's reference count to remain elevated and the object to never be freed.

To trigger the leak, you can compile & run the following program with ASan enabled:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <openssl/evp.h>
#include <openssl/rsa.h
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: bug severity: fips change severity: ABI change branch: 3.4 branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: Fix `EVP_KEYMGMT` leak in `evp_pkey_signature_init()` error paths

body_excerpt:
Fixes #29654 and #23612

The following QUIC transport parameters are now configurable via [`SSL_get_value_uint()` / `SSL_set_value_uint()`](https://docs.openssl.org/3.6/man3/SSL_get_value_uint/):
* `max_udp_payload_size`
* `initial_max_data`
* `initial_max_stream_data_{uni, bidi_local, bidi_remote}`
* `initial_max_streams_{uni, bidi}`
* `ack_delay_exponent`
* `max_ack_delay`
* `disable_active_migration`
* `active_connection_id_limit`

Moreover, the implementation now respects the peer-advertised `max_udp_payload_size`. Previously, the Maximum Datagram Payload Length (MDPL) was statically set to 1200 bytes. The sender now utilizes the full range allowed by the peer, subject to MTU constraints.

body_excerpt:
While it saddens me to fix anything about it as I believe I am possibly the world's biggest hater of the verify callback,

I think this is obviously wrong. the callback is encouraged by tradition which makes it ok, to call things like the undocumented X509_STORE_CTX_get0_current_crl function to look at things and decide to log things, have side effects, or change the verifier behaviour by overriding ok.

If it does this with the free calls happening before the callback is invoked, ctx->current_crl may be handed to it after having been free'd.

While you do need a lot of prayer and clean living if you use the verifier callback, let's at least not go out of our way to feed a use after free condition to it.

Noticed while reviewing one of t8m's changes.

body_excerpt:
This monster patch tries to enable cast-qual warning.

Note: my goal here is maily to stop introducing new const cast mistakes.

[370 files changed, 1255 insertions, 1195 deletions] - please read this per-patches.

As the code (and API) in many places requires a cast to a non-constant value,
I introduce the CONST_CAST() macro that silences this warning.

All these patches are written intentionally to do only simple changes,
not rewriting code, to minimize the possibility of introducing regressions.

A lot of code can be fixed later, it is _intentionally_ not done here.

There are several patches, split per directories:

- introduce CONST_CAST macro, add it to clang-format (otherwise the code
is reformatted in a very unreadable way) and enable the warning
The macro is defined in internal/common.h, all code must then use this header
(is there a better place?)

- Fix demos without use of CONST_CAST macro. To avoid an ugly double cast,
I made some arrays non-const.
(We should really have const version of PARAMS setters to solve it properly.)

- paramnames generated code does not use the macro, but directly generates
(type)(uintptr_t) cast

- then for each dir (apps, crypto, fuzz, includes, providers, ssl, test),
Two sets of patches are prepared
  - simple fixes adding/removing const where needed
  - masking the rest of the warnings with CONST_CAST

These patches can probably be joined, but IMO it is easier to read.

I could not avoid touching so
labels: severity: fips change extended tests no_news_changes_needed
title: Fix or workaround cast-qual warning and enable it as default

body_excerpt:
When `ZLIB_SHARED` is defined and DSO_load() fails to load the zlib library, `ossl_comp_zlib_init()` incorrectly returns 1 (success) while leaving all function pointers (`p_compress`, `p_uncompress`, etc.) as NULL.

This causes `COMP_zlib()` and `COMP_zlib_oneshot()` to return valid-looking COMP_METHOD pointers, but when these methods are used (e.g., during TLS 1.3 certificate decompression), the NULL function pointers are dereferenced, causing a SIGSEGV crash.

The bug occurs because the NULL pointer check (lines 297-303) was inside the `if (zlib_dso != NULL)` block, so it was skipped entirely when `DSO_load()` returned NULL.

The fix moves the NULL pointer check outside the conditional block, consistent with how `c_brotli.c` and `c_zstd.c` handle this case. Now if the DSO fails to load, all function pointers remain NULL, the check catches this, and the function correctly returns 0 (failure).

This also fixes an incorrect cast of `p_uncompress` from `compress_ft` to the correct `uncompress_ft` type.

PoC demonstrating the bug: https://github.com/SiteRelEnby/openssl-zlib-poc
title: Fix NULL pointer dereference when zlib DSO fails to load

body_excerpt:
When `ZLIB_SHARED` is defined and `DSO_load()` fails to load the zlib library, `ossl_comp_zlib_init()` incorrectly returns 1 (success) while leaving all function pointers (`p_compress`, `p_uncompress`, etc.) as NULL.

This causes `COMP_zlib()` and `COMP_zlib_oneshot()` to return valid-looking COMP_METHOD pointers, but when these methods are used (e.g., during TLS 1.3 certificate decompression), the NULL function pointers are dereferenced, causing a SIGSEGV crash.

The bug occurs because the NULL pointer check (lines 297-303) was inside the `if (zlib_dso != NULL)` block, so it was skipped entirely when `DSO_load()` returned NULL.

The fix moves the NULL pointer check outside the conditional block, consistent with how `c_brotli.c` and `c_zstd.c` handle this case. Now if the DSO fails to load, all function pointers remain NULL, the check catches this, and the function correctly returns 0 (failure).

This also fixes an incorrect cast of `p_uncompress` from `compress_ft` to the correct `uncompress_ft` type.

PoC demonstrating the bug: https://github.com/SiteRelEnby/openssl-zlib-poc

CLA: trivial

body_excerpt:
As mentioned by viktor, the desired behaviour is:

```
The current_crl is NULL when the running callback invocation is about errors unrelated to validation failures via a particular CRL a user may want to report the issuer of, ... 
The current_crl is (whenever possible) not NULL when reporting errors specifically related to that CRL.
```

the problem with this happens when we call check_crl with something that is not what current_crl is set to. we can potentially enter the time check code, and we then need to call the callback with the certificate that failed the time check which is not current_crl.

Correct this by removing the dance in the time check code, and always setting current_crl whenver we call check_crl.

This means that when we are considering a delta crl, we report the correct crl to the callback, instead of possibly handing them NULL (if they get called the time check clobbers it), or the non-delta crl (because we are looking at a delta while having current_crl set to crl - which was why we had the dance in the time code to begin with.  We don't need to change current_crl in the time check code if we always have current_crl set to the thing we are evaluting.

While we're here take a few minutes to document X509_STORE_CTX_get0_current_crl and warn that the returned
pointer is an internal value.

title: Ensure current_crl always points to the crl we are considering

body_excerpt:
and make ASN1_BIT_STRING_set_bit compute the unused bits of the  BIT_STRING.
    
The implicit trunction behaviour allows you to set a value without
keeping the unused bits consistent, using ASN1_STRING_set, and then
have it magically "fixed" to account for the unused bits in the last
octet on output.

As it turns out, after much searching, nothing is using this behavior,
  
As we now have the new ASN1_BIT_STRING_set1 to set the entire value
and keep the unused bits correct, we make ASN1_BIT_STRING_set_bit
also do the same. Now that both the setters change the object
correctly we remove the implicit trunctation in ossl_i2x_ASN1_BIT_STRING
and make the provided BIT_STRING argument const.

See discussion in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29185 and in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29117

For https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29117
Why not pile onto #30095 too, since github has decided to suck about issues..

body_excerpt:
(Your choice of G and A words)

This installs a global destructor if we have destructor support.

The global destructor does nothing and immediately returns under normal operation. If a global flag indicating that global cleanup is wanted, it does what OPENSSL_cleanup() used to do.

OPENSSL_cleanup() is then modified to set the global flag indicating that global cleanup is wanted. At this point if we have destructor support, it immeditely returns, and the action of the cleanup operation is done by the global destructor.

If we do not have destructor support, by default OPENSSL_cleanup will do nothing.  if DO_NOT_SKIP_OPENSSL_CLEANUP is defined at compile time, it will instead run the cleanup operation when called as it used to do.

This ensures that if we have destructor support, the actions of an OPENSSL_cleanup() requested by an application will only happen after any subordinate library destructors which could call into OpenSSL functions have already run, and
by default, if we do not have destructor support, we take the safe option of not doing anything.

body_excerpt:
So I am still not sure we even want to do this.

For it to make sense we have to have a goal of getting rid of them with an eye for the warning provides real benefit in the future and we aren't just casting it away to shut it up.

At the moment, I do not think we are there yet, and our API's are not const-correct enough for us to be there yet without extensive casting away of the problem, which ends up hiding the problem for the future.

I also really dislike macros to make this go away which change the base size of the pointer type, or anything else tricky.

So what this does, is turn on the warning, and introduces a couple of macros to selectively disable it in places we can't handle it yet.  It doesn't mess things up with casts.

I did a few examples of using this around the affected area, aside from that, I used it to disable it in the affected files.

To convince me turning this on would be useful right now, I'd need to be certain it has benefit, in that it made places where there was real risk that const data could be modified to where it would not be. That means not casting it away, that means API change to ensure you have const staying const.

There also needs to be a reasonable solution for free functions freeing const objects, that does not involve turning them into macros or anything else goofy

So what this is missing is a .md file (cast-fixing.md) that explains these macros, where to use them, and how to clean up so you don't need them, without ju

body_excerpt:
The n_fold() function in krb5kdf.c computes LCM(blocksize, constant_len) and then iterates from lcm - 1 down to 0. If constant_len is zero, lcm becomes zero, leading to lcm - 1 underflowing to UINT_MAX. Since the loop variable 'l' is signed (int), this causes undefined behavior during integer conversion, and even if handled, would result in an extremely long loop (DoS).

Although the KDF requires a non-empty protocol-derived constant per RFC 3961, Section 5.1, the implementation did not explicitly reject constant_len == 0. An empty octet string passed via OSSL_KDF_PARAM_CONSTANT could bypass the NULL check and trigger this condition.

Add an explicit check in KRB5KDF() to reject constant_len == 0 with PROV_R_INVALID_CONSTANT_LENGTH, alongside the existing upper-bound check.

This fixes a potential security vulnerability (DoS) and eliminates undefined behavior.

Signed-off-by: Anton Moryakov <ant.v.moryakov@gmail.com>
title: providers: implementations: kdfs: fix potential DoS and undefined behavior in KRB5KDF due to zero-length constant

body_excerpt:
The KDSA instruction can operate in 2 different modes:
- Deterministic mode - nonce 'k' is supplied by user.
- Non-deterministic mode - nonce 'k' is randomly generated by the instruction itself.

When running in the FIPS-Module, do not use KDSA's non-deterministic mode, but generate the nonce 'k' using OpenSSL's random number generator. This ensures that the nonce is generated using a FIPS-approved random number generator.

It also makes the FIPS KAT tests work, because those use a pre-setup deterministic random number generator to produce deterministic ECDSA signatures even for non-deterministic mode.

Fixes: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29713
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: bug severity: regression tests: exempted
title: s390x: EC: use OpenSSL's RNG for ECDSA nonce 'k' for FIPS module

As we are going to have several versions of openssl supporting providers, it makes sense to separate the folders for various versions.

Despite providers have a well-defined ABIs, it's not clear whether it would be safe to use non-self-contatined providers built against a particular version of libcrypto against the different version of openssl (e.g. because of atexit()-handler etc). Separating the providers per version resolves this problem.

## Summary

Clarify in the BN_add(3) documentation that the `ctx` parameter must not be NULL.

## Details

The documentation for BN_add and related functions (BN_mod, BN_mod_add, etc.) did not explicitly state that the `ctx` parameter cannot be NULL. Users may assume NULL is acceptable since some other OpenSSL functions allow it, but passing NULL to functions like BN_mod_add() or BN_mod() causes a crash because the code calls BN_CTX_start() and BN_CTX_get() without null checks.

## Summary
- Allow `pkcs12 -export` to read key and certificates in any order when using only `-in` (without `-inkey`)
- Previously the key had to appear before certificates in the input file

  - Cert-then-key: works (previously failed without `-inkey`)

## Example

Previously this would fail:
```bash
# Create file with cert before key
cat cert.pem key.pem > combined.pem
openssl pkcs12 -export -in combined.pem -out output.p12 -passout pass:test
# Error: No private key found
```

Now it works correctly.

body_excerpt:
## Summary

- Fixes RFC 8446 section 4.2 compliance for TLS extension alert codes
- Returns `illegal_parameter` (47) instead of `unsupported_extension` (110) when a TLS 1.3 ServerHello contains an extension valid for TLS 1.2 but not TLS 1.3 (e.g., `status_request`)
- Adds test case to verify correct alert behavior

## Problem

When parsing a TLS 1.3 ServerHello, `tls_collect_extensions()` uses a combined context (`SSL_EXT_TLS1_2_SERVER_HELLO | SSL_EXT_TLS1_3_SERVER_HELLO`) because the TLS version hasn't been determined yet. Extensions like `status_request` that are valid for TLS 1.2 ServerHello but not TLS 1.3 ServerHello would:
1. Pass the initial context check (valid for combined context)
2. Fail the unsolicited extension check with `SSL_AD_UNSUPPORTED_EXTENSION`

Per RFC 8446 section 4.2, this should return `illegal_parameter` instead.

## Solution

Skip the unsolicited extension check for extensions that are version-specific within the ServerHello context. This allows `tls_validate_all_contexts()` to handle them later with the correct `illegal_parameter` alert once the TLS version is known.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing tests pass (`make test TESTS="test_sslextension"`)
- [x] Added new test (Test 9) that injects `status_request` extension into TLS 1.3 ServerHello and verifies the client returns `illegal_parameter` (47)

Fixes #29353

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: ssl: Return illegal_parameter for TLS 1.3 invalid extensions per RFC 8446

body_excerpt:
## Summary

- Documents that providers must be unloaded before their associated library context is freed
- Adds warnings to both OSSL_PROVIDER(3) and OSSL_LIB_CTX(3) man pages

## Problem

When an application loads a provider into a library context and then cleans up by calling `OSSL_LIB_CTX_free()` before `OSSL_PROVIDER_unload()`, it triggers a heap-use-after-free. This order dependency was not documented.

## Changes

**OSSL_PROVIDER.pod** - Added to OSSL_PROVIDER_unload() description:
> When cleaning up, providers must be unloaded before their associated library context is freed with OSSL_LIB_CTX_free(). Calling OSSL_PROVIDER_unload() after OSSL_LIB_CTX_free() has been called on the associated library context results in undefined behavior.

**OSSL_LIB_CTX.pod** - Added to OSSL_LIB_CTX_free() description:
> Any providers that were explicitly loaded into ctx using OSSL_PROVIDER_load(3) or OSSL_PROVIDER_try_load(3) must be unloaded using OSSL_PROVIDER_unload(3) before calling OSSL_LIB_CTX_free(). Failure to do so results in undefined behavior.

## Test plan

- [x] POD syntax validated with `podchecker`
- [x] No nits reported by `util/find-doc-nits -n`

Fixes #27522

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: doc: document provider/library context cleanup order requirement

body_excerpt:
## Summary

- Clarifies that each SSL_CTX/SSL has its own certificate store (not a global store)
- Explains that multiple key/cert pairs of different types can be loaded
- Documents the purpose: offering different certificate types (RSA, ECDSA, etc.) on a single TLS server socket
- Explains automatic certificate selection during TLS handshake

## Problem

The existing documentation mentions that "the internal certificate store of OpenSSL can hold several private key/certificate pairs" but this wording was unclear and led developers to think it was a global library store rather than per-context storage. This caused TLS server developers to design their configuration syntax to only accept a single key/cert pair.

## Changes

Updated the NOTES section in SSL_CTX_use_certificate(3) with clearer explanation:

> Each **SSL_CTX** or **SSL** object has an internal certificate store that can
> hold multiple private key/certificate pairs at a time. This allows a TLS
> server to offer different certificate types (e.g., RSA, ECDSA, or post-quantum
> algorithms) on a single listening socket. During the TLS handshake, OpenSSL
> automatically selects the most appropriate certificate based on the negotiated
> cipher suite and the signature algorithms advertised by the client. To load
> multiple key/certificate pairs, simply call the certificate and private key
> loading functions multiple times with different key types.

## Test plan

- [x] POD syntax validated with `podchecker`
- [x] No nit
labels: approval: done branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: documentation tests: exempted branch: 4.0
title: doc: clarify that SSL_CTX/SSL can hold multiple key/cert pairs

body_excerpt:
## Summary

- Clarifies that `-verifyCAfile`, `-verifyCApath`, and `-verifyCAstore` should contain CA certificates
- Explains their role as "trust anchors" in client certificate verification
- Adds explicit warning about security implications of including root CAs

## Problem

The original documentation said these options take "trusted certificates" which was ambiguous. System administrators may not realize that including root CA certificates means any certificate issued under that entire PKI hierarchy would be accepted for client authentication, rather than just specific issuing CA certificates.

## Changes

**Before:**
> A file in PEM format CA containing trusted certificates to use for verifying client certificates.

**After:**
> A file in PEM format containing trusted CA certificates to use as trust anchors when verifying client certificates. Typically this file contains the specific issuing CA certificates that are authorized to issue client authentication certificates. If root CA certificates are included, then any certificate issued under that root CA's PKI hierarchy may be accepted for client authentication.

Similar clarifications were made to `-verifyCApath` and `-verifyCAstore`.

## Test plan

- [x] Generated POD file passes `podchecker` validation
- [x] No nits reported by `util/find-doc-nits -n`

Fixes #29584

## Summary

- Fixes inconsistent naming of algorithms and key types in EVP_PKEY-SLH-DSA(7)
- Separates algorithm names from key type identifiers for clarity

## Problem

The DESCRIPTION section had a mish-mash of naming conventions. Some entries had the "EVP_PKEY-" prefix and some didn't:

**Before:**
> The **SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s**, **EVP_PKEY-SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f**, **SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s**, **EVP_PKEY-SLH-DSA-SHA2-192f**...

This was inconsistent and the names with "EVP_PKEY-" prefix couldn't be used correctly in code.

## Changes

Rewrote the section to match EVP_PKEY-ML-DSA(7) format:

**After:**
> SLH-DSA implements the algorithms **SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s**, **SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f**... The corresponding key types **EVP_PKEY_SLH_DSA_SHA2_128S**, **EVP_PKEY_SLH_DSA_SHA2_128F**... are implemented in OpenSSL's default and FIPS providers.

Now clearly separates:
- Algorithm names with hyphens (e.g., `SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s`)
- Key type identifiers with underscores (e.g., `EVP_PKEY_SLH_DSA_SHA2_128S`)

X509V3_EXT_print() can call into unknown_ext_print(), which calls into BIO_dump_indent_cb(),
which can return -1 on failure.
Therefore, a -1 return for X509V3_EXT_print() or X509V3_EXT_print_fp() is possible.

I don't know where the docs for this live, but the statement on the e.g. OpenBSD man page isn't right: https://man.openbsd.org/X509V3_EXT_print.3 as it says

> X509V3_EXT_print() and X509V3_EXT_print_fp() return 0 if failure was both detected and considered relevant

This was detected using static analysis.

## Summary

The `X509_ATTRIBUTE` accessor functions were not const-correct, preventing callers from usefully interacting with a `const X509_ATTRIBUTE *`. This PR updates the following functions to accept `const X509_ATTRIBUTE *` and return const pointers:

- `X509_ATTRIBUTE_get0_object()` → returns `const ASN1_OBJECT *`
- `X509_ATTRIBUTE_get0_type()` → returns `const ASN1_TYPE *`

All callers have been updated to use const pointers for the return values.

Fixes #29811

body_excerpt:
… size misuse

Using sizeof(unsigned long) as SipHash digest size; SipHash supports only 8 or 16 bytes. On platforms where sizeof(unsigned long) == 4, the call fails, and lcid_hash returns the zero-initialized value, degrading the hash table into list.

The issue was kindly reported by Stanislav Fort at Aisle Research.

`OSSL_FN_sqr()` takes the square of on operand.  The result `OSSL_FN` may be of any size, and if it's smaller than the result of squaring the operand, the result is truncated to that size.

Finally, the BIGNUM 'sqr' function is wrapped around the OSSL_FN 'sqr' function

This change aims to prevent the `OSSL_HTTP_REQ_CTX_nbio` function from trying to parse an http response as ASN.1 (DER-encoded) content when the Content-Type header is `text/...`

As is, the code also checks that we received an http error (since this is the scenario discussed in the issue). But this does raise the question: should we also stop the processing on successful status code since expect_asn1 && Content-Type : text/... clearly contradict each other?

Since this is only a small change of behaviour (might only look like a change of error-message to the user of the function), I don´t know if this mandates changes in documentation/new tests. What are your thoughts?

Fixes https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29748

body_excerpt:
In providers/applications that create custom libctx'es via OSSL_LIB_CTX_new, its possible, if the default provider has never been initaialized during the lifetime of the linked libcrypto, that we actually wind up creating the default libctx when we free the aforementioned custom libctx via, as an example:
```
legacy_teardown->
 OSSL_LIB_CTX_free->
  ossl_lib_ctx_is_default->
   get_default_context->
    get_thread_default_context->
     default_context_do_init
```
While this isn't catastrophic, its needless, and in some cases has the potential to leak memory (for instance if a provider is loaded and unloaded repeatedly in an environment in which the provider is linked to libcrypto.so while the calling application is statically linked to libcrypto.a

Its also fairly easy to clean up, by adding an internal parameter to gate the creation of the default libctx on the request of the caller, so do that here

Fixes openssl/project#1846
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge severity: fips change
title: Don't setup a default context while tearing down private contexts

body_excerpt:
In the [providers] section it is now possible to inject an override of both the OPENSSL_MODULES and underlying built-in MODULESDIR default that applies to the immediately following providers, until another such override is added.

Because section name/value pairs are deduplicated by name, to apply multiple settings an option "<whatever>." unique prefix is supported.

    [providers]
    modulesdir = /some/where1
    providerA = ...
    providerB = ...
    1.modulesdir = /some/where2
    providerC = ...
    2.modulesdir =
    providerD =

Setting the value empty clears the override for the subsequent providers, until perhaps another override is added...

## Summary

- Documents the `-strict` command line option and `StrictCertCheck` configuration option in `SSL_CONF_cmd.pod`
- Previously these were documented only as "Enables strict mode protocol handling" without explaining what strict mode actually does

The new documentation explains that strict mode enforces:
- All certificates in the chain must use configured signature algorithms (no SHA1 fallback)
- CA certificate parameters are validated against the supported list (not just the end-entity certificate)
- For client authentication, certificate type must match server's CertificateRequest
- For client authentication, certificate issuer must be in server's CA list
- Notes that Suite B modes always imply strict mode

Fixes #16140

body_excerpt:
## Summary

Users reading the documentation for the `-<cipher>` option often misunderstand
the syntax. The notation `B<-I<cipher>>` renders as "-cipher" with "cipher" in
italics, leading users to think they should type `-cipher aes-128-cbc` when the
correct usage is `-aes-128-cbc` (the cipher name directly as the option).

See: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/787468/

This PR updates the documentation in:
- **openssl-genpkey**: Added explicit clarification and multiple examples
- **openssl-enc**: Added clarification about the option syntax
- **openssl-pkey**: Added clarification and fixed a typo ("and and" → "and")

All three now:
- Explicitly state that the cipher name is prepended with a hyphen
- Clarify that it's NOT an argument to a `-cipher` flag
- Reference `openssl list -cipher-algorithms` to discover available ciphers

Fixes #26089

- [x] Reviewed rendered man page output

## Summary

These functions and types were previously undocumented:

- **SSL_DANE**: Opaque type that holds DANE verification state for an SSL connection
- **SSL_get0_dane()**: Returns a pointer to the SSL_DANE structure for an SSL connection
- **X509_STORE_CTX_set0_dane()**: Associates DANE verification state with a verification context

These are primarily used by applications implementing custom certificate
verification callbacks via `SSL_CTX_set_cert_verify_callback()` that need to
support DANE TLSA (RFC6698/RFC7671) authentication.

### Changes

**SSL_CTX_dane_enable.pod:**
- Added `SSL_get0_dane` and `SSL_DANE` to NAME section
- Added `typedef struct ssl_dane_st SSL_DANE;` to SYNOPSIS
- Added `SSL_DANE *SSL_get0_dane(SSL *ssl);` to SYNOPSIS
- Added description for SSL_DANE and SSL_get0_dane()
- Added return value documentation for SSL_get0_dane()

**X509_STORE_CTX_new.pod:**
- Added `X509_STORE_CTX_set0_dane` to NAME section
- Added function prototype to SYNOPSIS
- Added description explaining the function's purpose
- Added SEE ALSO references to SSL_CTX_dane_enable(3) and SSL_get0_dane(3)
- Added HISTORY entry noting it was added in OpenSSL 1.1.0

Fixes #24536

## Summary
- Fix incorrect threading documentation in `BIO_s_dgram_pair(3)` man page
- Clarify that only `BIO_recvmmsg` and `BIO_sendmmsg` support simultaneous use by multiple threads
- Document that other BIO calls like `BIO_read`, `BIO_write`, etc. are not safe for concurrent use due to the BIO API storing state (such as retry flags) on the BIO object itself

As noted by @t8m in the issue, only `BIO_recvmmsg` and `BIO_sendmmsg` are supposed to be simultaneously usable. The previous documentation was misleading because even with internal locking, there's a race condition between operations like `BIO_read` and `BIO_should_retry`.

body_excerpt:
As we are already adding lists to verify param, as discussed, let's just separate out
the lists of things we wish to validate against the certificate cn in the subject

As a result X509_verify_cert no longer will care about the ALWAYS_CHECK_SUBJECT
or NEVER_CHECK_SUBJECT flags - you simply set what you want to do before calling it
by adding whichever names you want to check against the SAN DNSnames, and whichever
names you want to check against the CN 

The legacy X509_check_host and friends still do respect the flags, but as we have
not reccomended their use for ages, let's deprecate them.

Fixes: https://github.com/openssl/project/issues/1897

Long term, we need to enhance our own http3 demo client to support the download/resumption/etc features that we need for interop.  We're tracking that effort in:

OSSLINAPPL (OpenSSL Is Not A Public Portability Layer)
    
    I blame @nhorman and @sashan, who made me update the adjacent
    documentation, and it was sitting there like an unloved and
    ignored chronically ill pet with no hope for any future
    quality of life.

![libc](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8af58f0c-ef5c-4f83-9a56-b5e3cab9e571)

    
    Since this really does not need an alternative API or a
    deprecation stragedy we can simply remove it. Anyone
    who was using this can use libc atexit() if they must.

## Summary
Added a warning to the `BIO_get_data(3)` man page clarifying that `BIO_set_data()` should only be used with custom BIO types created via `BIO_meth_new(3)`.

Using `BIO_set_data()` on built-in BIO types like `BIO_s_socket()` or `BIO_s_file()` causes undefined behavior, typically resulting in crashes when `BIO_free()` is called, because the BIO's cleanup function will attempt to free the replaced pointer.

Fixes: #29645

body_excerpt:
* Use EVP\_\*\_CTX_set_params instead of legacy EVP\_\*\_CTX_ctrl. The next
  controls have been replaced with params:
  - EVP_PKEY_CTRL_SET_IV to OSSL_EXCHANGE_PARAM_KDF_UKM
  - EVP_PKEY_CTRL_CIPHER to OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_CIPHER
  - EVP_MD_CTRL_TLSTREE to OSSL_DIGEST_PARAM_TLSTREE
  - EVP_CTRL_TLSTREE to OSSL_CIPHER_PARAM_TLSTREE
* Use provider interface during TLS setup and routines
* Use EVP_PKEY_get0_type_name instead of legacy EVP_PKEY_get_id
* Don't translate EVP_PKEY_CTRL_PEER_KEY to params. Key exchange algorithms
  use this control with static keys which can't be achieved with encrypt
  context (only derive context supports setting peer key). This therefore
  results in the inability to use the next ciphersuites in some cases (see
  draft-chudov-cryptopro-cptls-04) with provider-implemented PKEYs:
  - TLS_GOSTR341094_WITH_28147_CNT_IMIT
  - TLS_GOSTR341001_WITH_28147_CNT_IMIT
  - TLS_GOSTR341094_WITH_NULL_GOSTR3411
  - TLS_GOSTR341001_WITH_NULL_GOSTR3411

body_excerpt:
Recently, curl dropped support for the openssl quic stack, which led to failures in our interop testing, as our quic interop tests relied on using curl with openssls quic stack to do http3 tests.

We put in an interim solution to fix this, by backing off our curl build in our test docker file to build curl at a commit level prior to the removal of that support.

This is the longer term solution.  If curl doesn't support the openssl quic stack, its no longer useful to us, so lets replace its use in our quic interop container to use our custom http3 client from demos/http3 instead.

This PR does 2 things:

1) Updates the ossl-http3-demo.c client to support:
   a) making multiple requests to a server (instead of the static single request to fetch "/" that was there before
   b) adds an option to specify a download directory to save the requested files to

The http3 interop tests requires both these bits of functionality to pass the test

2) Update the Dockerfile such that we no longer use curl to make these requests and instead use (1)

I've tested these changes against all the servers in the interop test that support http3 (including our own), and they all pass.

##### Checklist
- [x] tests are added or updated
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge
title: replace curl with openssl custom http3 client in quic interop tests

body_excerpt:
As far as I understand this code is unmaintained.

Many codepaths implemented are long obsolete, buggy, or had recent-ish
cryptoanalysis results showing it is insecure.

Also, many newer features and algorithms are not implemented for up to
date ciphers, modes, and extensions.

The code as is, does not test against any known alternative certified
implementations. At the same time, most popular consumer facing
website supports IETF/NIST prime256r1 curve now. Hence likely usage is
restricted to website that require a certified implementation instead
(such as CryptoPro).

Desite the NO_GOST ifdefs, actually many gost-specific code-paths are
not guarded with such ifdefs and have leaked into the shared libraries
ABI and in active libcrypto/libssl hotpaths, despite being effectively
no-op for the NO_GOST builds.

The useful portions of this code, that are still relevant and in use
could be reinstante, assuming there is a non-geoblocked TLS server
with which connectivity can be tested that is running uptodate
alternative implementation (for example CryptoPro); and that all
codepaths are correctly guarded with configuration features and
ifdefs, such as to not affect the abi of the NO_GOST builds. At
parity, with forexample NO_FIPS builds.

Potentially, design would be interesting to expose TLS extensions via
the provider, such that provider could do partial processing of the
certificate chain validation; and TLS extensions handling, such that
one could m
labels: branch: master approval: review pending triaged: feature
title: Remove obsolete and partial support for engine-only dstu and gost

body_excerpt:
our fuzzer caught this:
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/actions/runs/21625445341/job/62324333796

Overnight.

We're getting a heap buffer overrun in the SRTP KDF.

Its caused by the fact that the fuzzer will occasionally generate salt parameters that are very small, which passes the
OSSL_PARAM_get_octet_string function, but isn't long enough to be a valid salt.  Because of this, when we actually do the key derivation, the SRTPKDF function assumes the salt is long enough and blindly attempts to copy KDF_SRTP_SALT_LEN (14) bytes from the fetched parameter into a local buffer, resulting in an overrun.
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge severity: fips change
title: Fix buffer overrun in SRTPKDF

body_excerpt:
Fixes issue in Discussion 22861

Running the commandline with "openssl enc -id-aes256-wrap-pad ...." does not work for large private keys (keys are larger for PQ). This operation uses the cipher BIO (bio_enc) which has a fixed size buffer. Note that the encrypt was not failing but the decrypt operation did fail.
Investigating this issue uncovered the following issues:

1) AES_WRAP_PAD is documented as only working for non streaming cases
   (since all of the input is required). This meant that although
   encrypt did not fail, it could never pass on a subsequent decrypt.
   A flag has been added to track the update() and fail if it is called
   multiple times.

2) An internal function returned an int which could be negative and it
   was assigned to a size_t which ignored the error condition.

The size of the bio_enc buffer has been changed from 4K to 8K.

We expect a custom legacy method (e.g. RSA_METHOD) to work even if you have set if via RSA_set_method() on an RSA object that you assign to an EVP_PKEY. Such an object is imported into the default provider, and it should retain the
custom RSA_METHOD during that process. The same applies for other similar methods (DSA_METHOD, EC_KEY_METHOD, DH_METHOD).

In order for this to work we have to break the normal rules about passing complex objects across the provider boundary. But since this is just for the default provider we can get away with it.

Fixes #29702

body_excerpt:
Currently the state machine moves the read record layer to the next epoch base off of state events. The problem with this is when either the client or the server sent out a record during the handshake that causes an alert. If the state machine moved either the client or server to the next epoch it will not be able to handle that alert message. This approach delays moving the read record layer to the next epoch until we actually receive a record in that epoch. This allows us to process the alert messages.

Fixes: openssl/project#1786
Fixes: openssl/project#1785
Fixes: openssl/project#1759

body_excerpt:
We expect a custom legacy method (e.g. RSA_METHOD) to work even if you have set if via RSA_set_method() on an RSA object that you assign to an EVP_PKEY. Such an object is imported into the default provider, and it should retain the
custom RSA_METHOD during that process. The same applies for other similar methods (DSA_METHOD, EC_KEY_METHOD, DH_METHOD).

In order for this to work we have to break the normal rules about passing complex objects across the provider boundary. But since this is just for the default provider we can get away with it.

This is an alternative implementation to that shown in #29942. The difference here is that we pass the whole low level object across to the provider, rather than just the METHOD. This has the advantage then any associated ex_data is retained. Another difference is that I am passing the object across via a thread local variable rather than a param. This is because I need to do this in the "newdata" call - but that does not accept parameters, and I don't want to amend the provider API to support this.

Fixes https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29702
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: bug severity: fips change severity: regression tests: present
title: Enable custom low-level legacy methods to work even in the provider world (take 2)

Thus this is done by sending an Alert message via the Proxy.

The existing SSL_get_sigalgs() and SSL_get_shared_sigalgs() are not a good fit for TLS 1.3, because signature schemes are no longer generally combinations of separate digest and signing algorithms encoded in the two byte codepoint.

The new `SSL_get0_sigalg()` and `SSL_get0_shared_sigalg()` functions just report the signature scheme name and codepoint.

body_excerpt:
The idle test advances fake time by 10 ms per iteration for 6000 iterations,
simulating 60 seconds of idle.  However, the default QUIC idle timeout
(`QUIC_DEFAULT_IDLE_TIMEOUT`) is only 30 seconds.  Neither the client nor
the server configures a longer timeout, so the negotiated idle timeout is:

    min(client_default=30s, server_default=30s) = 30s

During the idle test, `SSL_handle_events()` is deliberately not called on
the client (to test thread-assisted mode), meaning the client relies
entirely on its background assist thread for keepalive.  Whether the
assist thread sends keepalive pings in time to reset the server's idle
deadline depends on scheduling—hence the intermittent nature.

## Fix

Three changes in `test/quic_tserver_test.c`, all guarded by
`use_thread_assist && use_fake_time` so non-idle-test iterations are
unaffected:

1. **Set server idle timeout to 90 s** — after `ossl_quic_tserver_new()`,
   before transport parameters are generated.

2. **Set client idle timeout request to 90 s** — after `SSL_new()`,
   before `SSL_connect()` triggers transport parameter generation.

   Together the negotiated timeout becomes `min(90s, 90s) = 90s`,
   comfortably exceeding the 60 s test duration.

3. **Periodic server-side keepalive** — every 100 iterations (= 1
   simulated second) during the idle loop, guarded by `is_connected`
   to avoid pinging a terminated channel.

## Testing

Ran `make TESTS='test_quic_tserver' test` repeatedly;
title: Fix intermittent quic_tserver_test failure during idle test

During the idle test, `SSL_handle_events()` is deliberately not called on the client (to test thread-assisted mode), meaning the client relies entirely on its background assist thread for keepalive. Reported by https://github.com/1seal

The index bound is now being checked.
I have shuffled code around so that:
1) Bounds checks that can be done early in the set_ctx_params are being done. The assumption in some cases is that they are initialized to zero.
2) Bounds checks that rely on multiple params being set should not be done in set_ctx_params. These need to be delayed until after the set, which is in the derive.
3) There is no assumption that the set_ctx_params() params are all set in one call, it is possible that the cipher could be done in a seperate call to setting the keylength (Probably not likely, but it should be possible).
4) I do the keylength check twice because of 3). In the case that the cipher was set after the keylength.
5) The index check moved down to inside the block where it is actually used,
6) Error codes have been added, which ensures the test cases are hitting the expected paths,
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: bug severity: fips change extended tests no_news_changes_needed

body_excerpt:
OSS-FUZZ caught a heap buffer overflow:
https://oss-fuzz.com/testcase-detail/6120637956685824

It occurs because, the srtpkdf derivation function requires that, if an index and label are provided, that a specific index length be available dependent on the label set.  However, the set_ctx_params function that precedes the derivation never checks for those preconditions, leading to the derivation function accessing the index array beyond the bounds set if the array is shorter than expected.

Fix it by adding a check to kdf_srtpkdf_set_ctx_params, specifically Check that if both the label and index are set, ensure that the length of the index array is exactly the expected amount of data (4 bytes for labels 3, 4, and 5 and 6 bytes for all other labels).

NOTE: We could relax this check somewhat, and just require those minimal lengths, and allow more, but since the derivation function only uses those exact lengths, it seems prudent to do an exact check.

Fixes openssl/project#1870


##### Checklist
- [x] tests are added or updated
labels: branch: master approval: review pending severity: fips change
title: Fix heap buffer overflow in SRTPKDF

body_excerpt:
`x25519_scalar_mult` and `fe_tobytes` may dispatch to assembly
implementations (e.g. `x25519-x86_64.s`) that MemorySanitizer
cannot instrument. As a result, output buffers written by the
assembly appear uninitialized to MSan, causing false positives
when subsequent code reads them (e.g. `CRYPTO_memcmp` in `ossl_x25519`).

This adds `__msan_unpoison` annotations after assembly writes in
`ossl_x25519` and `ossl_x25519_public_from_private`, following the
same pattern already used in `sha3.c`, `bn_intern.c`, and
`eng_rdrand.c`.

The issue was discovered while testing ClickHouse's SSH protocol
support with MSan: the libssh library calls `EVP_PKEY_derive` for
X25519 key exchange, and the shared secret buffer produced by the
assembly implementation triggers a false `use-of-uninitialized-value`
report in `BN_bin2bn`.
labels: hold: cla required branch: master approval: review pending triaged: bug severity: fips change
title: Add __msan_unpoison for X25519 assembly output buffers

body_excerpt:
Previously, a malformed ASN.1 INTEGER in the CRL Number or Delta CRL Indicator extension would cause a parse error but the CRL would not be explicitly rejected. Existing code discards the error and continues, accepting a CRL it cannot fully parse, unlike other libraries and implementations that reject the CRL outright.

Malformed encoding suggests a corrupt or tampered CRL, data that cannot be parsed cannot be trusted. Reject the CRL outright if either extension cannot be decoded, regardless of whether the extension is marked critical. This prevents silent soft-fail behavior where revoked certificates could pass validation unchecked.

Fixes #27374
labels: branch: master approval: ready to merge triaged: feature tests: present backlog fix
title: CRL: reject malformed CRL Number and CRL Delta Indicator

This is a rebase of the feature/ech branch. As such the PR is targetting *master* - but should be merged (with a force push) to the feature/ech branch.

The feature/ech branch has not been rebased for a while and I encountered issues due to the clang-format of master than has happened since the last rebase. In particular I had problems with pre-commit and the @esyr's script to do the reformat - which did not seem to interact well. The problem was the codespell was complaining about typos in the code and the script would then abort. The typos were subsequently fixed in a later commit, but by then it was too late. Possibly I could have somehow turned off pre-commit locally to avoid this, but instead I just fixed the typos directly in the commits that introduced them. This eventually meant that when I got to the "Fix typos in ECH implementation" commit - the commit was empty and got dropped.

Another problem I had was that after the rebase was complete I was not getting a clean compile when using "--strict-warnings". I suspect the warnings are now stricter on master?? I have added some fixup commits on the end of the rebase to fix the compilation errors.

Finally, I am now getting a test failure in the ech_corrupt test. I have not investigated the reason.
labels: approval: review pending triaged: feature severity: fips change tests: present severity: ABI change branch: feature no_news_changes_needed

body_excerpt:
OSS-FUZZ tripped over a timeout:
    https://issues.oss-fuzz.com/issues/477959320
    
It occurs because the pkcs12 data the fuzzer feeds into the mac
    verification routine requests a large number of iterations (I think gdb
    read it as 15346721 or some such), which causes very long processing
    times while verifying the mac.  This is something of an artificial
    problem unique to the fuzzer, as the fuzzer contains a 60 second timeout
    on any single test iteration.
    
Fix it by limiting the iteration count to 100 only when running the
    fuzzer tests.
    
Fixes openssl/srt#89

body_excerpt:
PR https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/30045

Fixed an oss-fuzz failure that occured because we feed random data into the pkcs12 kdf, which sometimes results in a huge iteration count, that leads to timeouts in oss-fuzz.

The fix was to simply limit the number of iterations that we go through during derivation.  This breaks the kdf of course, but it doesn't really matter during fuzzing, because we don't expect random input data to produce reasonable results, so no harm, no foul.

except.

We also, in our CI, build our fuzzer tests and run them through our regular CI unit tests, during which we both provide valid data, and expect valid results, and pr 30045 breaks that expectation.

The conventional wisdom is to simply skip unit tests that break under these sorts of conditions (we do this for things like 70-test_quic_record.t already).

however, the tests that broke here are 25_test_x509, 30_test_evp, 80_test_pkcs12, and 90_test_store_cases.  It seems like we would want to keep testing those unless we absolutely have to skip them.

So instead, lets indicate that we are running the unit tests with an environment variable, and check that variable when we have an UNSAFE_FOR_PRODUCTION build, skiping the iteration clamp in pkcs12kdf if it is.  This allows us to continue running these unit tests, while still getting the oss-fuzz runs to pass.

marking as urgent as this is breaking CI currently.

##### Checklist
- [x] tests are added or updated
labels: approval: done branch: master triaged: bug severity: urgent tests: exempted branch: 3.3 branch: 3.4 branch: 3.5 branch: 3.6
title: Fix unit tests when run under fuzz builds

This PR fixes one bug where the frozen method store cache was keyed only by algorithm name and property query, which could cause incorrect cache hits when different
operation types (e.g., KEM vs signature) share the same algorithm name

EVP_KEM and EVP_KEYMGMT implement freezing, and the performance for EVP_<KEM/KEYMGMT>_fetch should be on par with EVP_MD and EVP_CIPHER.

Resolves: https://github.com/openssl/project/issues/1876
Resolves: https://github.com/openssl/project/issues/1833
labels: approval: ready to merge triaged: feature severity: fips change tests: present branch: feature

<!--
Thank you for your pull request. Please review these requirements:

"Fixes #29931"

Problem:
On Windows, `nmake clean` attempts to remove `test\test-runs`,
but the actual directory created by the test suite is `test-runs`
at the repository root.

This results in clean reporting:
"The system cannot find the file specified"
while leaving the real test artifacts intact.

Solution:
Updated the Windows clean target to remove the correct directory:

rd /Q /S test-runs 2>NUL

The stderr redirection prevents unnecessary errors when the
directory does not exist, matching expected clean behavior.

Result:
`nmake clean` now correctly removes test artifacts.

body_excerpt:
FIXES: #29513 

In OpenSSL 3.5.4 when configured with no-scrypt tag, scrypt is still listed as available in these following commands:

`openssl list -key-managers`
`openssl list -public-key-algorithms`

We (@ocapraro @emmieono) solved this bug by adding #ifndef to `defltprov.c` and added tests to crosscheck the key managers and public key algorithms list with the disabled list.

## Before
```c
...
{ PROV_NAMES_SCRYPT, "provider=default", ossl_kdf_keymgmt_functions, PROV_DESCS_SCRYPT_SIGN },
...
```

## After
```c
...
#ifndef OPENSSL_NO_SCRYPT
    { PROV_NAMES_SCRYPT, "provider=default", ossl_kdf_keymgmt_functions,
        PROV_DESCS_SCRYPT_SIGN },
#endif
...
```

Checklist
- [X] tests are added or updated

when configuring with:

./Configure no-sskdf --strict-warnings

The build breaks as sskdf_new is defined but not used (as the same sskdf file is used to implement x931kdf with a different new dispatch function).  i.e. we will build the file when sskdf is disabled but x931kdf is enabled, omitting any use of sskdf_new

Easy fix, just gate the inclusion of sskdf_new on #ifndef OPENSSL_NO_SSKDF.

Fixes #30105
